[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 情史 - A term referring to a history of emotions or a chronicle of love and affection.
  • 情痴 - A person deeply infatuated with love or emotions.
  • 傾赤相與 - To be fully open and sincere with each other.
  • 吉凶同患 - To share both good and bad fortunes.
  • 奇穷奇枉 - Unusual misfortunes or injustices.
  • 求為之地 - To seek a way to help or assist.
  • 嗟嘆累日 - To sigh and lament for many days.
  • 中夜展轉不寐 - To toss and turn unable to sleep during the night.
  • 無情者 - A person who is unfeeling or indifferent.
  • 志言相忤 - To have differing opinions or beliefs.
  • 委曲以情導之 - To gently persuade or guide with emotion.
  • 作佛度世 - To become a Buddha to save the world.
  • 多情欢喜如来 - A Buddha whose name means 'the Buddha of Many Feelings and Joy.'
  • 信心奉持 - To have faith and uphold.
  • 恢诡非常 - Extraordinary and unconventional.
  • 情之可久 - The enduring nature of emotions.
  • 无情化有 - Transforming the absence of emotion into the presence of it.
  • 私情化公 - Transforming private emotions into public ones.
  • 浇俗 - A superficial or shallow custom.
  • 曲终之奏 - The final notes of a song or piece of music.
  • 广情 - To broaden one's emotional understanding.
  • 导欲 - To lead to desire or temptation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 詹詹外史 - The name of the author or compiler, indicating a possible pseudonym or pen name.
  • 多情欢喜如来 - A Buddhist concept where the Buddha is described as one who brings joy through compassion and love.
  • 无数喜神 - Imaginary beings representing joy and happiness.
  • 仇敌冤家 - Enemies and rivals, often used in the context of resolving conflicts through compassion.
  • 恢诡非常 - Describing something as extraordinary and unconventional, often used in the context of literature or art.
  • 情之可久 - The idea that true emotions are enduring and timeless.
  • 无情化有 - Transforming the absence of emotion into the presence of it, a concept related to the cultivation of empathy and compassion.
  • 私情化公 - Transforming private emotions into public ones, suggesting the idea of sharing and spreading emotional connections.
  • 浇俗 - Referring to a superficial or shallow social custom, often in contrast to deeper spiritual or emotional values.
  • 曲终之奏 - The final notes of a song or piece of music, often used metaphorically to describe the conclusion of a story or narrative.
  • 广情 - Broadening one's emotional understanding and empathy.
  • 导欲 - Leading to desire or temptation, often in a negative sense.

Proper Nouns

  • 詹詹外史 - The name of the author or compiler, indicating a possible pseudonym or pen name.
  • 多情欢喜如来 - A Buddhist concept where the Buddha is described as one who brings joy through compassion and love.
  • 无数喜神 - Imaginary beings representing joy and happiness.
  • 仇敌冤家 - Enemies and rivals, often used in the context of resolving conflicts through compassion.
  • 恢诡非常 - Describing something as extraordinary and unconventional, often used in the context of literature or art.
  • 情之可久 - The idea that true emotions are enduring and timeless.
  • 无情化有 - Transforming the absence of emotion into the presence of it, a concept related to the cultivation of empathy and compassion.
  • 私情化公 - Transforming private emotions into public ones, suggesting the idea of sharing and spreading emotional connections.
  • 浇俗 - Referring to a superficial or shallow social custom, often in contrast to deeper spiritual or emotional values.
  • 曲终之奏 - The final notes of a song or piece of music, often used metaphorically to describe the conclusion of a story or narrative.
  • 广情 - Broadening one's emotional understanding and empathy.
  • 导欲 - Leading to desire or temptation, often in a negative sense.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 天地若无情 - A philosophical statement suggesting that if heaven and earth were devoid of emotion, nothing would come into existence.
  • 一切物无情 - Extends the idea that all things being without emotion would prevent the cycle of life and death.
  • 生生而不灭 - Describes the continuous cycle of life and death, emphasizing that this cycle persists because of the presence of emotion.
  • 四大皆幻设 - Refers to the four elements (earth, water, fire, wind) as illusory constructs, a concept often found in Buddhist philosophy.
  • 性情不虚假 - Asserts that the nature and emotions of beings are genuine and not deceptive.
  • 有情疏者亲 - Suggests that those who are emotionally distant become closer, implying the transformative power of emotion.
  • 无情亲者疏 - Conversely, those who are emotionally close become distant, highlighting the opposite effect.
  • 我欲立情教 - Expresses the desire to establish a 'teaching of emotion,' a unique concept in the text.
  • 教诲诸众生 - Means to instruct all sentient beings, emphasizing the universal application of the teaching.
  • 子有情于父 - Describes the emotional bond between a child and a father, extending to other relationships.
  • 臣有情于君 - Describes the emotional bond between a subject and a ruler.
  • 推之种种相，俱作如是观 - Suggests that all relationships should be viewed through the lens of emotional connection.
  • 万物如散钱 - A metaphor comparing all things to scattered coins.
  • 一情为线索 - Suggests that emotion is the thread that ties everything together.
  • 散钱就索穿 - Imagines the scattered coins being strung together by the thread of emotion.
  • 天涯成眷属 - Suggests that even distant people can become family or close companions.
  • 若有赋害等 - Refers to acts of harm or violence.
  • 自伤其情 - Suggests that such acts harm the emotional connections between people.
  • 如睹春花发，齐生欢喜意 - Describes the joy that arises when one sees spring flowers blooming, symbolizing the positive emotional impact of beauty.
  • 盗贼必不作，奸宄必不起 - Suggests that if people are filled with joy and harmony, acts of theft and rebellion will not occur.
  • 佛亦何慈悲，圣亦何仁义 - Questions the necessity of the Buddha's compassion and the sage's righteousness if the world is already harmonious.
  • 倒却情种子，天地亦混沌 - Suggests that if the seed of emotion is removed, the world would revert to chaos.
  • 无奈我情多 - Expresses the frustration of having too much emotion.
  • 无奈人情少 - Expresses the frustration of others having too little emotion.
  • 愿得有情人，一齐来演法 - Expresses the wish for all emotionally connected people to come together and practice the teaching.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天地 - Refers to heaven and earth, fundamental concepts in Chinese philosophy and cosmology.
  • 一切物 - Refers to all things, encompassing the natural and human worlds.
  • 四大 - Refers to the four elements (earth, water, fire, wind), a concept from Buddhist philosophy.
  • 性情 - Refers to the nature and emotions of beings, a central theme in Confucian and Daoist thought.
  • 情教 - A unique concept in the text, suggesting a teaching centered on emotion.
  • 众生 - Refers to all sentient beings, a common term in Buddhist and Daoist texts.
  • 春花发 - Refers to the blooming of spring flowers, a symbol of renewal and beauty.
  • 盗贼 - Refers to thieves and robbers, common figures in classical Chinese literature.
  • 奸宄 - Refers to rebels and troublemakers, often used in political and moral discourse.
  • 佛 - Refers to the Buddha, a central figure in Buddhism.
  • 圣 - Refers to sages, revered figures in Confucianism.
  • 混沌 - Refers to chaos or disorder, a concept from Chinese mythology and philosophy.

Proper Nouns

  • 吴人龙子犹 - The author of the text, a classical Chinese writer.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 六經皆以情教也 - General statement meaning 'The Six Classics all teach through emotion.'
  • 俾勿作於涼，于是流注于君臣、父子、兄弟、朋友之間而汪然有余乎！ - Complex sentence meaning 'to prevent it from becoming cold or unfeeling, and thus it flows into the relationships of ruler and subject, father and son, brother and brother, friend and friend, and becomes abundant and overflowing.'
  • 種種俱足，或亦有情者之朗鑑，而無情者之磁石乎！ - Metaphorical statement: 'Everything is present, perhaps it is the clear mirror for those with emotion and the magnet for those without.'
  • 耳目不廣，識見未超，姑就睹記憑臆成書，甚愧雅裁，僅當諧史 - Modest disclaimer from the author, acknowledging the limitations of his knowledge and the humble nature of his work.
  • 後有作者，吾為裨谌，因題曰《類略》，以俟博雅者擇焉 - Final statement expressing that the author considers himself a mere assistant (bì chén) to future writers, and that the work is titled 《類略》 ('A Brief Classification'), to be judged by learned scholars.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 六經 - The Six Classics: 《易》 (Book of Changes), 《詩》 (Book of Poetry), 《書》 (Book of Documents), 《禮》 (Book of Rites), 《詩》 (Book of Songs), and 《春秋》 (Spring and Autumn Annals). These are the foundational texts of Confucian education.
  • 《易》尊夫妇 - The 《Yijing》 (Book of Changes) emphasizes the relationship between husband and wife.
  • 《詩》有《關雎》 - A reference to the poem 《Guan Ju》 from the 《Book of Poetry》, which is traditionally interpreted as a love poem and a symbol of proper romantic conduct.
  • 《禮》謹聘、奔之別 - The 《Book of Rites》 distinguishes between proper marriage (聘) and elopement (奔), showing the Confucian emphasis on ritual and propriety.
  • 異端之學 - A term used to refer to non-Confucian or heretical teachings, often associated with Daoism or Buddhism.
  • 鳏曠 - A state of being unmarried and childless, often associated with ascetic or monastic life.
  • 辟諸《詩》云興、觀、群、怨、多識 - A reference to the traditional interpretation of the 《Book of Poetry》 as having five functions or purposes.
  • 裨谌 - A term from the 《Book of Documents》 referring to a minor official who assists in drafting or suggesting ideas.
  • 博雅者 - Literally 'those with broad learning and refined culture,' used to refer to learned scholars or critics.

Proper Nouns

  • 六經 - The Six Classics: 《易》 (Book of Changes), 《詩》 (Book of Poetry), 《書》 (Book of Documents), 《禮》 (Book of Rites), 《詩》 (Book of Songs), and 《春秋》 (Spring and Autumn Annals).
  • 《關雎》 - A poem from the 《Book of Poetry》.
  • 《類略》 - The title of the text being discussed.
  • 裨谌 - A term from the 《Book of Documents》, referring to a minor assistant or advisor.
  • 博雅者 - A term used to describe learned or refined individuals.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 江南詹詹外史述 - Title meaning 'Narrative by the Scholar of Jiangnan (the South of the Yangtze).'
  • 目录 - Table of Contents or Index, a standard term in classical Chinese texts.
  • 卷一 情贞类 - Volume One: The Category of Emotion and Fidelity.
  • （以下夫妇节义） - The following are stories of marital loyalty and righteousness.
  • （以下贞妇） - The following are stories of chaste wives.
  • （以下贞妾） - The following are stories of chaste concubines.
  • （以下贞妓） - The following are stories of chaste courtesans.
  • 补遗 - Supplement or Addendum, used to denote additional entries.
  • （以下补贞妇） - The following are additional stories of chaste wives.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 江南 - Refers to the southern regions of China, particularly the area south of the Yangtze River, historically associated with culture and literary production.
  • 詹詹外史 - Likely a pseudonym or title of the compiler or narrator, with '外史' suggesting a literary or semi-fictional author.
  • 情贞类 - A classification common in classical Chinese literature, focusing on stories of love, loyalty, and moral integrity.
  • 鲁陶婴妻 - The wife of Tao Ying of Lu, known for her fidelity and moral strength.
  • 张美人 - A concubine of Emperor Zhang of Han, known for her virtue.
  • 皇甫规妻 - The wife of Huangfu Gui, a Han dynasty general, noted for her loyalty and moral integrity.
  • 绿珠 - A famous concubine of the Western Jin dynasty, known for her loyalty and tragic death.
  • 戚大将军妾 - A concubine of a general surnamed Qi, likely from the Han or later dynasties.
  • 徐君宝妻 - The wife of Xu Junbao, a figure from the Southern Song dynasty, known for her loyalty during the Mongol invasion.
  • 关盼盼 - A famous courtesan of the Tang dynasty, known for her poetry and loyalty to her patron.

Proper Nouns

  • 詹詹外史 - A title or pseudonym; no known historical figure with this name. Should be translated as 'The Scholar of Jiangnan' or 'Narrator of Jiangnan' to preserve the literary style.
  • 范希周 - Fan Xizhou – a name of a historical or semi-legendary figure.
  • 盛道 - Sheng Dao – a name of a person, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
  • 祝琼 - Zhu Qiong – a name of a woman, likely a chaste wife or virtuous figure.
  • 天台郭氏 - Guo of TianTai – a woman from the TianTai region, likely known for her virtue.
  • 罗敷 - Luofu – a famous figure from the Han dynasty, known for her beauty and virtue.
  • 李妙惠 - Li Miaohui – a name of a virtuous woman.
  • 卢夫人 - Lady Lu – a wife known for her fidelity.
  • 金三妻 - The wife of Jin San – a name that may be historical or fictional.
  • 申屠氏 - A woman of the Shentu clan – common in classical Chinese texts to denote a woman from a certain family.
  • 王世名妻 - Wife of Wang Shenming – likely a historical or literary figure.
  • 惠士玄妻 - Wife of Hui Shixuan – a name that may be historical or literary.
  • 从二姑 - A woman who followed her two aunts – likely a story of filial piety or loyalty.
  • 狄阿毛妻 - Wife of Di Amao – a name that may be historical or fictional.
  • 泖湖谢氏 - Lady Xie of Maohu – a woman from the Maohu area, likely known for her virtue.
  • 史五妻 - Wife of Shi Wu – a name of a person, likely a virtuous woman.
  • 王氏妇 - A woman of the Wang family – a common way to refer to a woman without a specific name.
  • 徐君宝妻 - Wife of Xu Junbao – a known historical figure from the Southern Song dynasty.
  • 邓廉妻 - Wife of Deng Lian – a name that may be historical or fictional.
  • 独腕尼 - A nun with only one arm – a likely fictional or semi-legendary figure.
  • 海昌董氏 - Lady Dong of Haichang – a woman from Haichang, likely known for her virtue.
  • 章纶母 - Mother of Zhang Lun – a woman known for her moral character.
  • 歌者妇 - A woman who was a singer – likely a courtesan or performer known for her virtue.
  • 美人虞 - Lady Yu, the Beauty – a name that may be historical or fictional.
  • 随清娱 - Sui Qingyu – a name that may be historical or literary.
  • 邺中妇人 - A woman from Ye – a historical city in Hebei, likely a figure known for her virtue.
  • 张宁妾 - Concubine of Zhang Ning – a name that may be historical or literary.
  • 绿珠 - Lu Zhu – a famous concubine of the Western Jin dynasty.
  • 戚大将军妾 - Concubine of General Qi – a name that may be historical or literary.
  • 张小三 - Zhang Xiaosan – a name that may be historical or literary.
  • 高娃 - Gao Wa – a name that may be historical or literary.
  • 杨娼 - Yang the Courtesan – a name that may be historical or literary.
  • 韩香 - Han Xiang – a name that may be historical or literary.
  • 关盼盼 - Guan Panpan – a famous courtesan of the Tang dynasty.
  • 李姝 - Li Shu – a name that may be historical or literary.
  • 沈真真 - Shen Zhenzhen – a name that may be historical or literary.
  • 齐锦云 - Qi Jinyun – a name that may be historical or literary.
  • 王四儿 - Wang Sier – a name that may be historical or literary.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 意外夫妻 - Describes couples who come together unexpectedly, often in unusual or extraordinary circumstances.
  • 老而娶者 - Refers to individuals who marry later in life.
  • 妻自择夫 - Indicates a situation where a wife chooses her husband, which was uncommon in traditional Chinese society.
  • 夫妇重逢 - Describes couples who reunite after being separated, often due to circumstances such as war, travel, or other disruptions.
  • 情私类 - Indicates a collection of stories about secret or illicit romantic relationships.
  • 以先私后配 - Refers to a relationship that begins as a secret affair before becoming a formal marriage.
  • 补遗 - Means 'supplementary material' or 'addenda,' referring to additional stories or entries that were added later.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 私而未及配者 - A phrase indicating a person who is private (not married) and has not yet been paired (matched).
  • 私会 - A private meeting, often implying a secret or illicit encounter.
  • 补遗 - Supplementary entries or additional information.
  • 补先私后配 - Supplementary entries indicating a person who was first private and then paired.
  • 情侠类 - A category of stories or texts that combine romantic and chivalrous elements.

  • 有仇家欲报之而无道 - Means 'there was an enemy who wanted to take revenge but had no way to do so.'
  • 劫其妻父 - Means 'he kidnapped his wife's father.'
  • 使要其女 - Means 'to make him bring his daughter.'
  • 父呼其女而告之 - Means 'the father called his daughter and told her.'
  • 女计念 - Means 'the daughter thought carefully.'
  • 不听，则杀父，不孝 - Means 'if she does not listen, then he will kill her father, which is unfilial.'
  • 听之，则杀夫，不义 - Means 'if she listens, then she will kill her husband, which is unjust.'
  • 欲以身当之 - Means 'she wanted to take it upon herself.'
  • 应曰：“诺，夜在楼上，新沐头，东首卧，则是矣。妾请开户俟。” - Means 'she replied, 'Yes, at night on the upper floor, newly washed head, lying with the head facing east, that is it. I will open the door and wait.'
  • 仇家至，断头持去，视之，乃其妻头也 - Means 'the enemy came, cut off her head and took it away, and when they looked, it was her wife's head.'
  • 仇家痛焉，遂释，不杀其夫 - Means 'the enemy was moved by this, so he released him and did not kill his husband.'
  • 此女不忍其夫，宁自忍也 - Means 'this woman could not bear her husband, rather than herself.'
  • 郑雍姬之见偏矣哉 - Means 'Zheng Ouyi's view is biased indeed.'
  • 罗敷 - The name of the heroine.
  • 邯郸秦氏女，名罗敷 - Means 'a woman from the Qin family in Handan, named Luo Fu.'
  • 嫁邑人王仁 - Means 'she married a local man named Wang Ren.'
  • 仁为赵王家令 - Means 'Wang Ren was an official in the Zhao kingdom.'
  • 敷出采桑于陌上 - Means 'Luo Fu went out to pick mulberry leaves on the road.'
  • 赵王登台见而悦之 - Means 'the King of Zhao saw her from the tower and was pleased with her.'
  • 因置酒欲夺焉 - Means 'so he set up a banquet to take her.'
  • 敷善弹筝 - Means 'Luo Fu was good at playing the zheng (a Chinese zither).'
  • 作《陌上桑》之歌以自明 - Means 'she composed the song 'On the Roadside Mulberry Tree' to make herself known.'
  • 赵王乃止 - Means 'the King of Zhao then stopped.'
  • 日出东南隅，照我秦氏楼 - Means 'The sun rises in the southeast, shining on my Qin family tower.'
  • 秦氏有好女，自名为罗敷 - Means 'The Qin family has a beautiful daughter, who calls herself Luo Fu.'
  • 罗敷喜蚕桑，采桑城南隅 - Means 'Luo Fu likes silkworms and mulberry leaves, picking them in the south corner of the city.'
  • 青丝为笼系，桂枝为笼钩 - Means 'She uses green silk for the basket's ties and osmanthus branches for the basket's handle.'
  • 头上倭堕髻，耳中明月珠 - Means 'On her head is a wavy hairstyle, and in her ears are bright moon pearls.'
  • 缃绮为下裙，紫绮为上襦 - Means 'Her skirt is made of yellow silk, and her jacket is made of purple silk.'
  • 行者见罗敷，下担捋髭须 - Means 'Passersby see Luo Fu and put down their loads, stroking their beards.'
  • 少年见罗敷，脱帽着帩头 - Means 'Young men see Luo Fu and take off their hats, putting on turbans.'
  • 耕者忘其犁，锄者忘其锄，来归相怨怒，但坐观罗敷 - Means 'Farmers forget their plows, and hoeing men forget their hoes; when they return, they blame each other, just because they were watching Luo Fu.'

  • 使君遣吏往 - Means 'the Governor sent an official to go.'
  • 問是谁家姝 - Means 'to ask whose beautiful daughter she is.'
  • 素氏有好女，自名为罗敷 - Means 'the family of Su has a beautiful daughter who calls herself Luo Fu.'
  • 罗敷年几何 - Means 'How old is Luo Fu?'
  • 二十尚不足，十五颇有余 - Means 'She is not quite twenty but more than fifteen.'
  • 使君谢罗敷 - Means 'the Governor asked Luo Fu.'
  • 宁可共载不 - Means 'Can I ride with you?'
  • 罗敷前致辞 - Means 'Luo Fu stepped forward to speak.'
  • 使君一何愚 - Means 'How foolish you are, Governor!'
  • 使君自有妇，罗敷自有夫 - Means 'The Governor has his own wife, and I have my own husband.'
  • 东方千余骑，夫婿居上头 - Means 'In the east, there are over a thousand riders, and my husband is at the head of them.'
  • 何用识夫婿 - Means 'How do you recognize my husband?'
  • 白马从骊驹 - Means 'A white horse follows a black steed.'
  • 青丝系马尾，黄金络马头 - Means 'Blue silk ties the horse's tail, and gold ornaments the horse's head.'
  • 腰中鹿卢剑，可值千万余 - Means 'A sword in his belt, worth thousands of coins.'
  • 十五府小吏，二十朝大夫，三十侍中郎，四十专城居 - Means 'At fifteen, he was a minor official; at twenty, a court scholar; at thirty, a palace attendant; at forty, he ruled a city.'
  • 盈盈公府步，冉冉府中趋 - Means 'He walks gracefully in the public office, moving slowly and steadily.'
  • 坐中数干人，皆言夫婿殊 - Means 'Among the many people present, all say my husband is exceptional.'
  • 极慕己容色之美 - Means 'Extremely admiring her own beauty.'
  • 画出一个风流佳婿 - Means 'Portraying a charming and handsome husband.'
  • 夫妇相爱之情，隐然言外 - Means 'The affection between husband and wife is subtly implied.'
  • 赵王闻之，亦不觉惭矣 - Means 'When the King of Zhao heard this, he was also ashamed.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 李大惊曰 - Common way to introduce a character's reaction; 'Li was greatly surprised and said.'
  • 扬州卢举人 - Refers to a deceased person; 'The scholar-gentleman Lu from Yangzhou.'
  • 其死已久 - Indicates the passage of time since his death; 'He has been dead for a long time.'
  • 尔欺我也 - Expresses disbelief or suspicion; 'You are deceiving me.'
  • 隶备述如所谕语 - Indicates the servant's role in relaying information; 'The servant reported everything as instructed.'
  • 叩父母及妻名 - A way to verify identity; 'Asked about the names of his parents and wife.'
  • 一一不爽 - Confirms accuracy; 'Not a single one was wrong.'
  • 掩泣曰 - Indicates emotional distress; 'Covered his face and wept, saying.'
  • 其我夫矣 - Expresses recognition; 'It must be my husband.'
  • 始吾闻歌已疑之 - Indicates initial doubt; 'At first, when I heard the song, I suspected it.'
  • 恨未有间 - Expresses missed chances; 'Regretted not having an opportunity.'
  • 今日商偶往娼院 - Indicates a coincidental event; 'Today, the merchant happened to go to a brothel.'
  • 母亦过邻舟 - Indicates another coincidental event; 'His mother also passed by the neighboring boat.'
  • 依期舟来 - Indicates a planned meeting; 'Came to the boat as scheduled.'
  • 接李至公馆 - Indicates a formal meeting place; 'Brought Li to the public hall.'
  • 夫妻欢会如初 - Indicates a joyful reunion; 'The couple reunited happily as before.'
  • 商赀具付母主其出入 - Indicates financial and administrative responsibilities; 'The merchant handed over the funds and entrusted the mother to manage the affairs.'
  • 母转以委李 - Indicates a transfer of duties; 'The mother then entrusted these responsibilities to Li.'
  • 检视，历历分明 - Indicates thorough verification; 'When he checked, everything was clear and detailed.'
  • 封志完固 - Indicates the preservation of items; 'The seals were intact and secure.'
  • 关羽昔逃归汉 - Reference to a historical event; 'In the past, Guan Yu escaped back to Han.'
  • 曹公时不追 - Indicates a strategic decision; 'Cao Cao did not pursue him.'
  • 彼各为其主 - Indicates loyalty and allegiance; 'They were each serving their own lord.'
  • 贞妇也，可置之 - Indicates respect for her virtue; 'She is a chaste woman; she can be left alone.'
  • 弘治二年也 - Indicates the time period; 'It was the second year of the Hongzhi era.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 盧下帷發愤 - Metaphorical expression meaning Lu studied diligently in seclusion.
  • 不必绝家音 - Literally 'not necessarily cut off family communication,' meaning he maintained family ties.
  • 不必汲汲嫁妇 - Literally 'not necessarily rush to marry a woman,' meaning there was no urgency to marry.
  • 不必强纳士人之妻 - Literally 'not necessarily force to marry a scholar's wife,' meaning no pressure to marry a scholar's wife.
  • 矢心不贰 - Literally 'swore her heart without hesitation,' meaning she remained loyal and steadfast.
  • 遂成一片佳话 - Literally 'thus became a beautiful story,' meaning their story became a well-known and admired tale.
  • 病且死 - Literally 'sick and about to die,' describing a critical condition.
  • 君年少 - Literally 'you are young,' referring to the young wife.
  • 善事后人 - Literally 'treat future husbands well,' meaning to take care of herself and possibly future partners.
  • 剔一目示玄龄 - Literally 'plucked out one eye to show Xuanling,' a dramatic act of commitment.
  • 明无他念 - Literally 'showing no other thoughts,' meaning she had no other intentions.
  • 礼之终身 - Literally 'treated her respectfully for life,' meaning he honored her throughout his life.
  • 至妒 - Literally 'extremely jealous,' describing the wife's possessiveness.
  • 媵妾之流 - Literally 'the category of concubines,' referring to secondary wives or concubines.
  • 司空年暮 - Literally 'the Master of Works (a high-ranking official) is old,' referring to the husband's advanced age.
  • 优诏 - Literally 'preferential edict,' meaning a special decree or favor.
  • 执意不回 - Literally 'firmly refused to change her mind,' meaning she was resolute.
  • 若宁不妒而生，宁妒而死 - Literally 'If you prefer not to be jealous and live, or be jealous and die,' a dramatic choice presented to her.
  • 酌卮酒与之 - Literally 'gave her a cup of wine,' a symbolic gesture.
  • 若然，可饮此鸩 - Literally 'if so, you can drink this poison,' a metaphorical threat.
  • 实非鸩也 - Literally 'it was not actually poison,' revealing the deception.
  • 无所留难 - Literally 'without hesitation,' meaning she drank the wine without resistance.
  • 我尚畏见，何况玄龄 - Literally 'I am still afraid to see it, how much more so Xuanling,' expressing the emperor's fear of the wife's reaction.
  • 怕妇 - Literally 'afraid of women,' a colloquial term for a man dominated by his wife.
  • 感剔目之情 - Literally 'moved by the act of plucking out the eye,' referring to the emotional impact of the wife's gesture.

  • 媵妾 - A secondary wife or concubine, often of lower status than the primary wife.
  • 优诏 - A special decree or favor, often used to reward or honor individuals.

  • 妾猶及見夫方殂 - 'I still saw my husband die.'
  • 誰云妾無子 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Who says I have no son?'
  • 側室生兒與夫似 - 'My concubine bore a son who resembles my husband.'
  • 兒讀書，妾辟(纟盧) - 'My son reads, and I weave (or mend) the cloth.'
  • 空房夜夜聞啼烏 - 'In the empty room, I hear the cawing of crows every night.'
  • 兒能成名妾不嫁，良人瞑目黃泉下 - 'If my son can achieve fame, I will not remarry; my husband can rest in peace in the underworld.'
  • 一見之情，勝於百年 - 'The affection from a single encounter surpasses a lifetime of love.'
  • 且不怨納妾而能誨子，閨中大聖賢也 - 'Moreover, not resenting the taking of a concubine and being able to educate her son, she is a great sage in the inner chambers.'
  • 歌者婦 - 'The singing woman.'
  • 南中有大帥 - 'In the southern region, there was a great leader.'
  • 世襲爵位 - 'He inherited his title by hereditary succession.'
  • 歌婦色美 - 'The singing woman was beautiful.'
  • 與其夫自北而至 - 'She and her husband came from the north.'
  • 帥聞而召之 - 'The leader heard of her and summoned her.'
  • 每入，輒與其夫偕 - 'Every time she entered, she would accompany her husband.'
  • 更唱迭和，曲有餘態 - 'They would alternate singing and harmonizing, with the music having an extra charm.'
  • 帥欲私之，婦拒不許 - 'The leader wanted to have an affair with her, but she refused.'
  • 帥密遣人害其夫 - 'The leader secretly sent someone to kill her husband.'
  • 而置婦於別室，多其珠翠 - 'He placed her in a separate room and gave her many jewels and ornaments.'
  • 以悅其意 - 'To please her.'
  • 逾年，往詣之，婦亦欣然接待 - 'After a year, he went to see her, and she received him happily.'
  • 情甚婉孌 - 'Their feelings were very tender.'
  • 及就榻，袖中忽出白刃 - 'When they got to the bed, she suddenly pulled out a white blade from her sleeve.'
  • 擒帥欲殺之 - 'She grabbed the leader and wanted to kill him.'
  • 帥驚逸，婦逐之 - 'The leader fled in terror, and the woman pursued him.'
  • 遇二奴闔其扉，乃免 - 'She encountered two servants who closed the door, and he escaped.'
  • 旋使人執之，已自斷其頸矣 - 'Soon after, he had someone arrest her, but she had already cut her own neck.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 妾 - Refers to a concubine or secondary wife.
  • 側室 - Refers to a concubine or secondary wife.
  • 辟(纟盧) - Refers to weaving or mending cloth, a common task for women in ancient times.
  • 黃泉 - Refers to the underworld or the afterlife.
  • 南中 - Refers to the southern regions of China.
  • 大帥 - Refers to a high-ranking military leader.
  • 世襲爵位 - Refers to a hereditary title or rank.
  • 歌婦 - Refers to a woman who sings or performs music.
  • 珠翠 - Refers to precious jewels and ornaments.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • Who says I have no husband? I still saw my husband die. Who says I have no son? My concubine bore a son who resembles my husband. My son reads, and I weave (or mend) the cloth. In the empty room, I hear the cawing of crows every night. If my son can achieve fame, I will not remarry; my husband can rest in peace in the underworld. The affection from a single encounter surpasses a lifetime of love. Moreover, not resenting the taking of a concubine and being able to educate her son, she is a great sage in the inner chambers. The singing woman. In the southern region, there was a great leader. He inherited his title by hereditary succession. The singing woman was beautiful. She and her husband came from the north. The leader heard of her and summoned her. Every time she entered, she would accompany her husband. They would alternate singing and harmonizing, with the music having an extra charm. The leader wanted to have an affair with her, but she refused. The leader secretly sent someone to kill her husband. He placed her in a separate room and gave her many jewels and ornaments. To please her. After a year, he went to see her, and she received him happily. Their feelings were very tender. When they got to the bed, she suddenly pulled out a white blade from her sleeve. She grabbed the leader and wanted to kill him. The leader fled in terror, and the woman pursued him. She encountered two servants who closed the door, and he escaped. Soon after, he had someone arrest her, but she had already cut her own neck.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 此女中高渐离也 - Metaphorical comparison to Gao Jianli, emphasizing the woman's qualities.
  • 惭离为友，此为夫 - Literary device juxtaposing friendship with marriage, emphasizing the close relationship.
  • 祖龙之杀荆卿也，宜也 - Historical context: Qin Shi Huang killing Jing Ke was appropriate or justified.
  • 歌者之死，不更冤乎! - Rhetorical question emphasizing the injustice and tragedy of the singer's death.
  • 颈且可断，岂珠翠所能媚哉! - Hyperbole expressing that true value lies beyond material wealth.
  • 金兀术爱一小卒之妻，杀卒而夺之，宠以专房 - Historical event involving Wujie killing a soldier to take his wife and making her his favorite concubine.
  • 欲为夫报仇耳! - Desire to avenge one's husband.
  • 术嘿然，麾使去 - Wujie's reaction: silent and gesturing to dismiss someone.
  • 即日大享将士，召此妇出，谓曰 - Banquet where the woman is called out to make a choice.
  • 任汝于诸将中自择所从 - Giving the woman the choice to select her new husband from among the generals.
  • 此妇亦大有意思 - Characterization of the woman as having a strong character or intention.
  • 惜乎不肯拼一死也 - Regret that the woman did not choose to die instead of submitting to the new arrangement.
  • 然则为歌者妇愈难矣 - Being a wife of a singer is even more difficult due to added pressures and expectations.
  • 美人虞 - Introduction of a beautiful woman named Yu.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 力拔山兮气盖世 - Grandiose expression meaning 'to have the strength to uproot mountains and the spirit to surpass the world,' often used to describe heroic or powerful individuals.
  • 时不利兮骓不逝 - Lamentation meaning 'the times are not favorable, and the black steed will not move forward,' where '骓' (zhuī) refers to a black steed, symbolizing the speaker's own fate.
  • 可奈何 - Expression of helplessness or resignation, meaning 'what can be done?'
  • 虞兮虞兮奈若何 - Poignant cry meaning 'O Yu, O Yu, what shall we do with you?' where '虞' (yú) is a proper name, likely referring to a beloved person.
  • 汉兵已略地 - Statement meaning 'the Han forces have already occupied the land,' indicating the military situation.
  • 四面楚歌声 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the sound of Chu songs from all sides,' suggesting the encirclement and defeat of the Chu forces.
  • 大王意气尽 - Phrase meaning 'the king's spirit is exhausted,' indicating the loss of morale.
  • 贱妾何聊生 - Statement meaning 'a lowly concubine has no reason to live,' expressing despair.
  • 丈夫只手把吴钩 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'a man wields a sword alone,' symbolizing martial prowess.
  • 因何铁石打成心性 - Rhetorical question meaning 'why did he have a heart of iron and stone,' questioning the speaker's emotional hardness.
  • 却为花柔 - Statement meaning 'but he became tender like a flower,' contrasting with the previous hardness.
  • 一怒使人愁 - Statement meaning 'his anger makes people sad,' indicating the impact of his mood.
  • 只因撞着虞姬戚氏 - Statement meaning 'only because he met Yu and Qi,' referring to two significant women in his life.
  • 豪气都休 - Statement meaning 'his heroic spirit is gone,' indicating the loss of his former glory.
  • 项籍并刘季 - Refers to Xiang Ji (Xiang Yu) and Liu Ji (Liu Bang), two historical figures in Chinese history.
  • 喑哑吒咤 - Describes a person who is silent but fierce, often used to describe a warrior.
  • 千人自废 - Statement meaning 'a thousand men are rendered useless,' indicating overwhelming power.
  • 婉顺得其欢心 - Statement meaning 'gentle and compliant, winning his favor,' describing a person's behavior.
  • 死为舞草 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to die and become a dancing grass,' symbolizing a tragic end.

  • 虞美人草 - Term meaning 'Yu's beautiful woman grass,' referring to a specific type of flower or plant.

  • 急時舞草變鴛鴦 - In times of urgency, the plant transforms into mandarin ducks, symbolizing a change in fortune or situation. '舞草' refers to a type of plant that moves when touched, and '變鴛鴦' means 'turn into mandarin ducks.'

  • 之死靡他 - Until death, without changing, indicating unwavering loyalty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 绿珠 - The name 'Green Pearl' reflects the cultural significance of pearls in the Yue region.

  • 明君 - 'Bright Lady' refers to a historical figure, Wencheng Princess, who was sent to Tibet as a princess to promote cultural exchange.
  • 懊恼曲 - 'Annoyed Tune' is a type of musical composition, possibly reflecting emotional or narrative content.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 石崇之破，虽自绿珠始，亦其来有渐矣 - Complex construction linking Shi Chong's downfall to the incident with Green Jade and the gradual buildup leading to it.
  • 常刺荆州，劫夺远使，沈杀客商，以致巨富 - Long sequence of actions describing Shi Chong's accumulation of wealth through raiding Jingzhou, robbing foreign envoys, drowning and killing merchants.
  • 又遗王恺鸩鸟，共为鸩毒之事 - Specific incident involving Shi Chong and Wang Kai, where Shi Chong sent a poisonous bird to Wang Kai.
  • 有此阴谋 - Summary of the plot described in the previous clause.
  • 又以每邀燕集，令美人行酒，客饮不尽者，使黄门斩美人 - Detailed description of a cruel practice at Shi Chong's feasts, where beautiful women served wine and were killed if guests did not finish their wine.
  • 王丞相导与大将军敦，尝共访崇 - Introduction of the visit of Wang Dao and Wang Dun to Shi Chong.
  • 丞相素不能饮，辄自勉强，至于沉醉 - Description of Wang Dao's behavior at the feast, where he forced himself to drink until he was drunk.
  • 至大将军，故不饮，以观其气色 - Description of Wang Dun's behavior at the feast, where he did not drink to observe Shi Chong's demeanor.
  • 已斩三人，丞相劝敦使尽 - Outcome of the feast, where three women were killed, and Wang Dao advised Wang Dun to continue.
  • 敦曰：“彼自杀人，与我何与!” - Direct quote from Wang Dun, expressing his indifference to the killings.
  • 君子曰：“祸福无门，惟人所召。” - A gentleman's saying, emphasizing that good or evil is summoned by people.
  • 崇心不义，过杀人，焉得无报也! - Statement that Shi Chong's unjust heart and excessive killings would lead to retribution.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 兰堂上客至 - Poetic expression meaning 'guests arrive at the fragrant hall,' where '兰堂' (lán táng) refers to a beautiful or fragrant hall, and '上客' (shàng kè) means esteemed guests.
  • 绮席清弦抚 - Poetic description of a luxurious setting with fine silk mats and the sound of a clear zither being played. '绮席' (qǐ xí) means a beautiful or luxurious mat, and '清弦抚' (qīng xián fǔ) refers to playing a zither with a clear tone.
  • 自作《明君辞》 - '自作' (zì zuò) means 'composed by oneself,' and '《明君辞》' (míng jūn cí) is a poem or ode praising a wise ruler.
  • 还教绿珠舞 - '还教' (hái jiào) means 'also taught' or 'further taught,' and '绿珠舞' (lǜ zhū wǔ) refers to the dance performed by the famous courtesan Green Pearl.
  • 绛树摇欢扇 - Poetic image of a red tree (likely a metaphor for a beautiful woman) waving a fan of joy. '绛树' (jiàng shù) means a red tree, and '摇欢扇' (yáo huān shàn) means waving a fan of joy.
  • 金谷舞筵开 - '金谷' (jīn gǔ) refers to the famous Jin Garden, and '舞筵开' (wǔ yán kāi) means the dance hall is open or the dance begins.
  • 罗袖拂归客 - '罗袖' (luó xiù) means silk sleeves, and '拂归客' (fú guī kè) means gently brushing past the returning guests.
  • 留欢醉玉杯 - '留欢' (liú huān) means to prolong the joy, and '醉玉杯' (zuì yù bēi) means to get drunk from drinking from a precious cup.
  • 绿珠衔泪舞 - '衔泪' (xián lèi) means holding back tears, and '舞' (wǔ) means dancing.
  • 孙秀强相邀 - '孙秀' (sūn xiù) is a historical figure, and '强相邀' (qiáng xiāng yāo) means to forcibly invite.
  • 假天之报怨 - '假天' (jiǎ tiān) means to take advantage of heaven, and '报怨' (bào yuàn) means to take revenge.
  • 枭夷之立见 - '枭夷' (xiāo yí) means to be executed publicly, and '立见' (lì jiàn) means to be seen immediately.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有小妮子发前事 - A phrase meaning 'a young woman revealed past events.'
  • 纳兵往攻之 - Means 'sent soldiers to attack her.' The term '纳兵' is a bit archaic and can be translated as 'sent soldiers' or 'dispatched troops.'
  • 而一卒不令出 - Means 'but a single soldier was not allowed to go out.' The term '卒' refers to a soldier.
  • 恐有泄者 - Means 'fearing someone might reveal it.' The term '泄' means to reveal or leak.
  • 孝廉急属一卒逾重墙报公 - Means 'the filial and upright official urgently instructed a soldier to climb over the high wall and report to the public.'
  • 公召诸将问计 - Means 'the official summoned the generals to ask for advice.'
  • 或曰：“愿以死迎敌。” - Means 'some said, 'I am willing to die to meet the enemy.' The term '愿以死' means 'willing to die.'
  • 或曰：“早避之便。” - Means 'others said, 'It is better to avoid it early.' The term '早避之便' means 'it is better to avoid it early.'
  • 皆非也 - Means 'none of them are right.' The term '皆非也' is a common phrase used to dismiss all previous suggestions.
  • 乃自袒跣，跪迎夫人 - Means 'he then bared his body and knelt to welcome his wife.' The term '袒跣' means to bare one's body and walk barefoot.
  • 诸姬披发席藁，各抱其子请死 - Means 'the concubines untied their hair, sat on straw mats, and each held their child, asking to die.' The term '席藁' means to sit on straw mats.
  • 而请以子尝刃 - Means 'and asked to have their children try the blade.' The term '尝刃' means to test the blade.
  • 夫人令抱儿起，皆送还家 - Means 'the wife ordered them to pick up their children and send them home.' The term '令' means to order.
  • 首祸是老奴 - Means 'the one who started the trouble is the old slave.' The term '首祸' means the one who started the trouble.
  • 令杖之 - Means 'ordered him to be beaten.' The term '杖' means to beat with a stick.
  • 公即伏受，杖数十 - Means 'the official immediately submitted and was beaten dozens of times.' The term '伏受' means to submit and accept.
  • 门外将卒喊声大举，乃已 - Means 'outside, the generals and soldiers shouted loudly, and then it stopped.' The term '大举' means to shout loudly.
  • 箠挞诸姬最毒，罢归 - Means 'the beating of the concubines was the most severe, and then they returned home.' The term '箠挞' means to beat severely.
  • 由是公不得轻出 - Means 'from then on, the official could not leave easily.' The term '轻出' means to leave easily.
  • 既与姬绝，令尽箧其所有，各从所适 - Means 'after being separated from the concubine, he ordered her to pack all her belongings and go wherever she wished.' The term '箧' means a box or chest.
  • 诸姬计曰：“弃妾非主人意，何忍违之。” - Means 'the concubines said, 'Abandoning the concubine is not the master's intention, how can we bear to disobey.' The term '计' means to plan or say.
  • 乃轻装适他郡，披剃为尼，匿女僧家，梵诵至十余年 - Means 'they then dressed lightly and went to another prefecture, shaved their heads, became nuns, and hid in a nunnery, reciting scriptures for more than ten years.' The term '披剃' means to shave one's head.
  • 夫人殁，始归，各拥其子 - Means 'after the wife died, they returned, each with their child.' The term '殁' means to die.
  • 然诸姬子，夫人皆子之，亡恙 - Means 'but the children of the concubines, the wife treated them as her own, and they were all safe.' The term '亡恙' means to be safe or unharmed.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 乃扁舟下江 - Classical expression meaning 'he then took a small boat down the river,' indicating a departure or journey.
  • 直造杨氏之庐 - Means 'went directly to Yang's house,' describing a purposeful and direct action.
  • 登堂拜主母 - Means 'ascended the hall to pay respects to the mistress,' indicating a formal visit or greeting.
  • 捧杨首大恸 - Means 'embraced Yang's head and wept bitterly,' expressing deep grief or sorrow.
  • 悉出向所赠珠玑器具 - Means 'took out all the jewelry and utensils previously given,' indicating the use of accumulated wealth or gifts.
  • 以为资妆 - Means 'as dowry,' specifying the purpose of the items.
  • 又出仪物筵设之费 - Means 'also provided the costs for ceremonial items and feasts,' indicating additional expenses for formal events.
  • 为二子纳室 - Means 'arranged marriages for the two sons,' specifying the action taken for the sons.
  • 留侍汤药者一年 - Means 'stayed to care for Yang for a year,' indicating a period of devoted service.
  • 杨郁郁心恚以死 - Means 'Yang died from deep resentment,' describing the cause of death.
  • 妓又脱簪珥殡之 - Means 'the courtesan then removed her hairpins and ornaments to bury him,' indicating a symbolic act of mourning.
  • 守其柩不去 - Means 'remained by the coffin without leaving,' describing a steadfast commitment.
  • 姊院中衣食自丰 - Means 'sister, your quarters are well-provided for,' indicating a comfortable living situation.
  • 何为困此与我同辛苦 - Means 'why suffer here with me in hardship,' expressing a plea for relief.
  • 姊非碌碌市门女也 - Means 'sister, you are not an ordinary woman of the market,' indicating a higher status or dignity.
  • 少有不污之誓 - Means 'young, I had a vow of purity,' expressing a personal commitment.
  • 与主君交往廿载 - Means 'for twenty years with my master,' indicating a long-standing relationship.
  • 名虽风尘 - Means 'though my name is in the world of courtesans,' acknowledging a social status.
  • 身固不异杨氏之少房也 - Means 'my body is no different from Yang's young wife,' expressing a sense of equality or similarity.
  • 且主君为我而死 - Means 'and my master died for me,' indicating a deep bond.
  • 何忍背之 - Means 'how could I abandon him,' expressing loyalty.
  • 愿从主母侧 - Means 'I wish to serve by the side of the mistress,' indicating a desire to continue service.
  • 执庖湢之劳 - Means 'to perform the duties of cooking and cleaning,' specifying the tasks.
  • 殁且不悔 - Means 'even in death, I would not regret,' expressing a steadfast commitment.

  • 其戲也，可拒 - Literal: 'when he plays, it can be resisted.' Likely means the person's playful behavior can be avoided or ignored.
  • 其謝也，可原 - Literal: 'when he apologizes, it can be pardoned.' Suggests that his apologies can be forgiven.
  • 姝不多一死乎 - Literal: 'Can the beauty not die once?' Rhetorical question implying that the woman should consider death as a possible resolution.
  • 死而為厲，又甚矣 - Literal: 'To die and become a spirit of vengeance, that is even worse.' Indicates that death would lead to a more severe consequence.
  • 此女大有性气 - Literal: 'This woman has great character.' Could also imply she is strong-willed or volatile.
  • 宜王爱之不终也 - Literal: 'It is fitting that the king's love for her did not last.' Suggests that her character led to the end of her relationship with the king.
  • 虽然姝挟其素宠 - Literal: 'Although the beauty clings to her usual favor.' Implies she relies on her past favor with the king.
  • 意王必不终绝我 - Literal: 'She believes the king will not completely abandon her.' Shows her confidence in her relationship with the king.
  • 至挺刃相逼，而转思昔日怜香借玉之态，何可复得 - Literal: 'When faced with the threat of a sword, she thinks back to the tender ways of the past, which can no longer be regained.' Indicates regret over lost affection.
  • 悔而且怨，惟有一死以报同州而已 - Literal: 'Regretful and resentful, she can only die to repay her loyalty to the same state.' Suggests her only option is to die in loyalty.
  • 张受人之托，乃欺以私乱之，死其分也，何必厉 - Literal: 'Zhang, entrusted by someone, deceived her with private affairs, and her death is her fate, why should she become a spirit of vengeance.' Indicates that her death was her destiny due to the deception.

  • 夫妇其最近者也 - Husband and wife are the closest of all relationships.
  • 无情之夫，必不能为义夫，无情之妇，必不能为节妇 - A man without feeling cannot be a righteous husband, and a woman without feeling cannot be a chaste wife.
  • 世儒但知理为情之范，孰知情为理之维乎 - Scholars of the world only know that reason is the framework for emotion, but who knows that emotion is the foundation of reason?
  • 男子顶天立地，所担者具咫尺之义，非其所急 - Men stand tall and strong, but what they bear is but a small measure of righteousness, not their most urgent concern.
  • 吾是以详于妇节，而略于夫义也 - Therefore, I elaborate on the virtue of women and gloss over the righteousness of men.

  • 夫奔者，以情奔也 - Elopement is driven by emotion.
  • 奔为情，则贞为非情也 - If elopement is driven by emotion, then chastity is not driven by emotion.
  • 又况道旁桃柳，乃望以岁寒之骨乎 - How much more so for a peach or willow by the roadside, which hopes to remain strong in cold weather?
  • 春秋之法，使夏变夷，不使突变夏 - According to the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, it is acceptable to change the barbarians into the civilized, but not to change the civilized into the barbarians.
  • 圭而抱妇之志焉，妇之可也 - If a man is upright and has the intention of being a good husband, then he can be a good husband.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 补遗 - A term used in classical Chinese texts to indicate additional or supplementary material; can be translated as 'Supplement' or 'Appendix.'
  • 夫死，守志不二 - Means 'after her husband's death, she remained steadfast in her resolve'; the idiom '守志不二' emphasizes unwavering loyalty and fidelity.
  • 悲夫黄鹄之早寡 - A lamentation about the early widowhood of a mandarin duck (黄鹄), symbolizing the sorrow of losing a mate; '悲夫' is an expression of deep sorrow or lamentation.
  • 七年不双 - 'Seven years without a mate,' referring to the long period of widowhood.

  • 寡妇念此，泣下数行 - 'The widow thinks of this and sheds several tears,' indicating her emotional response.
  • 呜呼悲哉!死者不可忘 - 'Alas, how sad! The dead cannot be forgotten,' a lamentation about the permanence of death.
  • 飞鸟尚然，况于其良 - 'Even birds still remember their mates, how much more so for a faithful wife,' using a comparison to emphasize the widow's loyalty.
  • 虽有贤雄，终不可重行 - 'Even if there were a worthy husband, she would not remarry,' reaffirming her commitment to remain faithful.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 今人但知金谷，而罕知后凉 - Contrasts the well-known Jin Dynasty's 'Jingu' with the lesser-known 'Houliang' period. '但知' means 'only know,' and '罕知' means 'rarely know.'
  • 美人不获与绿珠并传 - '美人' refers to a beautiful woman, and '绿珠' (Lu Zhu) is a famous courtesan from the Jin Dynasty. '并传' means 'to be mentioned together.'
  • 香名寂寂，遗恨千古 - '香名' refers to a good reputation, '寂寂' means 'silent' or 'forgotten,' and '遗恨千古' means 'to leave a regret for all time.'
  • 岂贞姬烈女，亦有幸有不幸耶 - '岂' is a rhetorical particle meaning 'how could it be that,' '贞姬烈女' refers to virtuous and loyal women, and '亦有幸有不幸耶' means 'also have fortune and misfortune.'
  • 义妇张氏，济南邹平人 - '义妇' refers to a woman of virtue, '济南' is the name of a city, and '邹平' is the name of a county.
  • 年十八，归戍卒李午 - '年十八' means 'eighteen years old,' '归' means 'to marry,' and '戍卒' refers to a soldier stationed far from home.
  • 午同从子零出戍七闽 - '同从子' means 'a cousin,' '出戍' means 'to be sent to serve,' and '七闽' refers to a region in southern China.
  • 张独事舅姑父母，生养死葬无遗礼 - '张独事舅姑父母' means 'Zhang alone took care of her in-laws and parents,' '生养死葬' means 'supporting them in life and burying them after death,' and '无遗礼' means 'without any omission of proper rites.'
  • 枯骨未知所归 - '枯骨' means 'dry bones,' and '未知所归' means 'do not know where they are.'
  • 卧冰上，呼天祝曰 - '卧冰上' means 'lying on the ice,' and '呼天祝曰' means 'calling out to heaven and praying.'
  • 妾夫何罪!生既不见父母，死又不能归葬父母之旁 - '妾夫何罪' means 'what sin did my husband commit,' '生既不见父母' means 'in life, I did not see my parents,' and '死又不能归葬父母之旁' means 'in death, I cannot bury him beside my parents.'
  • 天若许妾取夫骨，虽寒甚，当得不死 - '天若许妾取夫骨' means 'if heaven allows me to take my husband's bones,' '虽寒甚' means 'even if it is very cold,' and '当得不死' means 'I should not die.'
  • 逾月竟不死 - '逾月' means 'more than a month,' and '竟不死' means 'still did not die.'
  • 乡人异之，为闻于县 - '乡人异之' means 'the villagers were amazed by her,' and '为闻于县' means 'they reported her to the county.'
  • 给过所遣之 - '给过所' means 'issued a pass,' and '遣之' means 'sent her.'
  • 至闽，零犹在 - '至闽' means 'arrived in Min,' and '零犹在' means 'Ling was still there.'
  • 问夫葬地，则榛莽日塞，不可识 - '问夫葬地' means 'asked about her husband's burial place,' '则榛莽日塞' means 'the area was overgrown with thorny bushes,' and '不可识' means 'could not be recognized.'
  • 张哀恸几绝 - '张哀恸' means 'Zhang mourned,' and '几绝' means 'almost to the point of death.'
  • 夫忽降于童，与张语生前事，甚悲，且示骨在处 - '夫忽降于童' means 'her husband suddenly appeared to a child,' '与张语生前事' means 'and spoke to Zhang about his life,' '甚悲' means 'very sad,' and '且示骨在处' means 'and showed where his bones were.'
  • 张如其言，发得之，持骨祝曰 - '张如其言' means 'Zhang followed his instructions,' '发得之' means 'and found the bones,' '持骨祝曰' means 'held the bones and prayed.'
  • 尔信妾夫耶!入口当融如冰雪，粘如胶 - '尔信妾夫耶' means 'do you believe it is my husband,' '入口当融如冰雪' means 'when put in my mouth, it should melt like ice and snow,' and '粘如胶' means 'and stick like glue.'
  • 果然 - Means 'as expected.'
  • 官异之，为上于大府，请复其家，使零护归济南 - '官异之' means 'the officials were amazed,' '为上于大府' means 'reported to the governor,' '请复其家' means 'requested to restore her family,' and '使零护归济南' means 'sent Ling to escort her back to Jinan.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 蔡侯誉息夫人之美 - The Duke of Cai praised the beauty of the Lady of Xi. This phrase sets the tone for the story, indicating that the Lady of Xi is highly regarded for her beauty.
  • 楚子灭息，以息妫归 - The King of Chu conquered Xi and took the Lady of Xi as his wife. This is a key event that sets the plot in motion.
  • 既生二子，犹未言 - After giving birth to two sons, she still remained silent. This indicates a significant character trait of the Lady of Xi.
  • 吾一妇人，而事二夫，纵不能死，其可笑言 - I, a woman, have served two husbands. Even if I cannot die, how can I laugh? This is a poignant statement that reveals the Lady of Xi's emotional state.
  • 楚为之兴兵破蔡 - Chu then raised an army and defeated Cai. This shows the consequences of the Lady of Xi's silence.
  • 张生 - Zhang Sheng. '生' can also mean 'to give birth' or 'to be born'.
  • 家启酒肆，颇为赡足 - He ran a tavern and was quite well-off. This provides background information about Zhang Sheng's life.
  • 绍兴辛巳冬，虏骑南下，淮人率奔京口 - In the winter of Xin Si during the Shaoxing era, the northern cavalry advanced southward, and the people of Huai fled to Jingkou. This sets the historical context.
  • 张素病足，不能行，泊驻扬州 - Zhang had a chronic foot ailment and could not walk, so he stayed in Yangzhou. This explains why Zhang did not flee with the others.
  • 卓氏，为夷酋所掠 - Zhuo, his wife, was captured by a barbarian chieftain. This introduces a new conflict.
  • 我夫在城中，蓄银五锭，必落他手，不若同往取之 - My husband is in the city, and he has five ingots of silver. They will surely fall into others' hands. Why don't we go together to retrieve them? This shows Zhuo's cunning plan.
  • 酋喜，偕诣张处，逼夺之 - The chieftain was pleased and went with Zhuo to Zhang's place, where they forcibly took the silver. This describes the execution of the plan.
  • 张戟手恨骂 - Zhang angrily cursed them with his hands. This shows Zhang's reaction.
  • 以为悦己，凡掳获金帛，悉以委之，相托如真夫妇 - He thought Zhuo was pleasing him, so he entrusted all the gold and silk he had captured to her, treating her as if she were his true wife. This reveals the chieftain's trust in Zhuo.
  • 卓痛饮酋酒，醉卧，投利刃断其喉，席卷财物，鞭马访张 - Zhuo drank heavily with the chieftain, fell asleep drunk, and then stabbed him in the throat with a sharp knife. She took all the valuables and rode a horse to find Zhang. This is the climax of the story.
  • 张话前事，责数，欲行决绝 - Zhang told Zhuo about what had happened and scolded her, wanting to break off the relationship. This shows Zhang's initial reaction to Zhuo's actions.
  • 卓取所携付之曰 - Zhuo took what she had brought and gave it to him, saying... This is a turning point in the story.
  • 当时不设此计，渠必不肯信我。今日之获，乃张本于昔也 - If I hadn't thought of this plan, he would never have trusted me. Today's gain is due to what I did in the past. This is Zhuo's justification for her actions.
  • 于是闻者交称焉 - Thus, those who heard about it all praised her. This shows the public's reaction to Zhuo's actions.

  • 形中而白，且無須也 - Describes the groom as having a medium build, fair complexion, and no beard, providing a detailed physical description.
  • 得適人乎 - Question asking if the daughter will be married to someone suitable, expressing concern about the match.

  • 吾女今夕得適人乎 - Question asking if the daughter will be married tonight, expressing hope and anticipation.

  • 寧不驚而奔乎 - Rhetorical question asking if one would not be shocked and run away, expressing the natural reaction to the unusual appearance.
  • 素與盧相善 - Phrase indicating that Zheng Ren has always been friendly with Lu, emphasizing the personal relationship.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 雇买小婢曰张二姐 - Hired a young maid named Zhang Erjie. Introduces the main character.
  • 虽无恶疾，而形体枯悴，肌肤皴皵，绝可憎恶 - Although she had no serious illness, her body was emaciated, her skin rough and cracked, making her extremely ugly. Describes Zhang Erjie's physical condition.
  • 姑使执庖爨舂汲之役 - The grandmother made her do the work of cooking, washing, and fetching water. Describes the tasks assigned to Zhang Erjie.

  • 乃命二姐为供给洗靧 - So he ordered Zhang Erjie to take care of his washing and grooming. Describes the tasks assigned to Zhang Erjie.
  • 盖以其寝陋，无所置嫌 - Because she was so ugly, there was no reason to feel embarrassed about her. Explains why Zhu assigned these tasks to Zhang Erjie.
  • 俄而刘亦谢退 - Soon after, Liu also left. Indicates Liu Yimin's departure.
  • 后十余岁，朱赴试省闱 - After more than ten years, Zhu went to take the provincial examination. Describes Zhu's later life.
  • 因诣市肆 - He happened to be at a market. Describes the setting of the encounter.
  • 闻有人呼声，回顾之，原不识面 - He heard someone calling out and turned to look, but he didn't recognize the person. Describes the initial encounter.
  • 其人力邀至所居 - The person insisted on inviting him to his home. Describes the invitation.
  • 具公服，再拜，叙至曩契 - He put on formal attire and bowed twice, recounting their past acquaintance. Describes the formal greeting.
  • 乃逸民也 - It was Liu Yimin. Reveals the identity of the person.
  • 盖以其寝陋，无所置嫌 - Because she was so ugly, there was no reason to feel embarrassed about her. Explains why Zhu assigned these tasks to Zhang Erjie.
  • 自违离之始，无人负书笈，偶值此妇，遂与之偕行 - From the time we parted, I had no one to carry my books, and I happened to meet this woman, so I traveled with her. Explains Liu Yimin's situation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 谚云 - Colloquial expression meaning 'a proverb says' or 'as the saying goes.'
  • 热油拌苦菜，自家心里爱 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'mixing hot oil with bitter vegetables, it's something one loves in one's own heart.' Metaphorical expression referring to the idea that something unpleasant or challenging can be enjoyable or cherished in its own way.
  • 业已相得 - Means 'have already become familiar' or 'have already gotten along well.'
  • 即王谢姬姜，弗与易矣 - Hyperbolic expression, using the prestigious Wang and Xie families to emphasize the value or uniqueness of the relationship or situation described. 'Even if they were Wang or Xie's daughters-in-law, they would not be exchanged.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 王谢 - Refers to the Wang and Xie families, two prominent clans during the Eastern Jin Dynasty. This allusion suggests a high social status or exceptional quality.
  • 姬姜 - Refers to the daughters-in-law of the Wang and Xie families, often used to denote women of high social status.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 谚云：“热油拌苦菜，自家心里爱。” - Direct quotation of a proverb, which must be rendered as such in English.
  • 业已相得，即王谢姬姜，弗与易矣。 - Elliptical sentence structure, understood as 'Having already become familiar, even if they were Wang or Xie's daughters-in-law, they would not be exchanged.'

Proper Nouns

  • 王谢 - Refers to the Wang and Xie families, two prominent clans during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
  • 姬姜 - Refers to the daughters-in-law of the Wang and Xie families, often used to denote women of high social status.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 莫使青春空岁月 - A warning not to let one's youth go to waste; '青春' (qīng chūn) means youth, and '空岁月' (kōng suìyuè) means to waste time.
  • 可怜和氏璧无瑕 - A reference to the famous jade, the He Shi Bi, which was flawless; '可怜' (kě lián) can mean 'how admirable' or 'how precious'.
  • 何事楚君终不识 - A rhetorical question asking why the king of Chu did not recognize the value of the He Shi Bi; '何事' (hé shì) means 'why' or 'what reason', and '终不识' (zhōng bù shí) means 'ultimately failed to recognize'.
  • 奇笑曰 - '奇笑' (qí xiào) means 'strange laughter' or 'unusual laugh', suggesting a reaction that is unexpected or out of place.
  • 否弟果女子也 - A direct statement meaning 'No, I am indeed a woman'; '否弟' (fǒu dì) is a colloquial way of saying 'no' or 'not', and '果女子也' (guǒ nǚzǐ yě) means 'indeed a woman'.
  • 面发赤 - '面发赤' (miàn fā chì) means 'face turning red', often due to embarrassment or shame.
  • 情同骨肉 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'as close as brothers and sisters', emphasizing a deep familial bond.
  • 俯就之意 - '俯就' (fǔ jiù) means 'to lower oneself' or 'to accept a lesser position', and '之意' (zhī yì) means 'the intention'; together, it suggests a willingness to accept a lower status.
  • 昔为兄弟，今为夫妇 - A statement indicating a change in relationship from siblings to spouses; '昔' (xī) means 'formerly', and '今' (jīn) means 'now'.
  • 恩义两全 - '恩义' (ēn yì) means 'gratitude and righteousness', and '两全' (liǎng quán) means 'both fulfilled'; together, it suggests a harmonious balance of gratitude and moral duty.
  • 安身之地 - '安身' (ān shēn) means 'to settle' or 'to find a place to live', and '之地' (zhī dì) means 'place'; together, it means 'a place to settle'.
  • 厝先灵 - '厝' (cuò) means 'to bury' or 'to place', and '先灵' (xiān líng) means 'ancestors' spirits'; together, it means 'to bury the ancestors' spirits'.
  • 家事尚微 - '家事' (jiā shì) means 'family affairs', and '尚微' (shàng wēi) means 'still small' or 'still insignificant'.
  • 决无更娶之理 - '决无' (jué wú) means 'definitely not', and '更娶之理' (gèng qǔ zhī lǐ) means 'reason to remarry'; together, it means 'there is definitely no reason to remarry'.
  • 三家坟墓 - '三家' (sān jiā) means 'three families', and '坟墓' (fén mù) means 'tomb' or 'grave'; together, it means 'tomb of three families'.
  • 弃此而去 - '弃此' (qì cǐ) means 'to abandon this', and '而去' (ér qù) means 'and leave'; together, it means 'to abandon this and leave'.
  • 恝然 - '恝然' (jiá rán) means 'indifferently' or 'without concern'.
  • 侍箕帚 - '侍' (shì) means 'to serve', and '箕帚' (jī zhǒu) refers to household chores; together, it means 'to serve by doing household chores'.
  • 奉三姓香火 - '奉' (fèng) means 'to serve' or 'to offer', and '三姓香火' (sān xìng xiāng huǒ) means 'the ancestral worship of three surnames'; together, it means 'to offer ancestral worship to three surnames'.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 和氏璧 - The He Shi Bi is a famous jade that was considered flawless and highly valuable; the story of the He Shi Bi is often used to illustrate the theme of recognizing true value.
  • 楚君 - Refers to the king of Chu, a state during the Warring States period; the reference to the king of Chu not recognizing the value of the He Shi Bi is a metaphor for failing to appreciate true worth.
  • 三义村 - The name of the village where the story takes place, named after the three brothers who are the main characters of the story.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 方之题词，近于自衒 - ‘方之题词’ refers to the inscription or preface written by Fang. ‘近于自衒’ is an idiom meaning ‘nearly amounts to self-promotion’ or ‘is close to self-advertisement.’ It carries a slightly negative connotation, suggesting the inscription is self-serving.
  • 真闺杰也 - ‘闺杰’ is a compound term meaning ‘outstanding woman from the inner quarters,’ i.e., a remarkable woman from the domestic sphere. It is a respectful and somewhat poetic way to refer to a woman of exceptional talent or virtue.
  • 端人 - A classical term meaning ‘upright person’ or ‘virtuous individual.’ It often refers to someone of moral integrity, even if not fully enlightened or wise.
  • 騃汉 - A term used to describe someone who is simple-minded, naive, or foolish. It can carry a condescending or affectionate tone depending on context.
  • 贞女 - A term for a ‘chaste woman’ or ‘virtuous maiden.’ It emphasizes moral purity and fidelity, especially in marriage.
  • 诚亦巧人 - ‘诚’ means ‘indeed’ or ‘truly,’ while ‘巧人’ means ‘clever person’ or ‘crafty person.’ Together, it implies that although she is virtuous, she is also shrewd or resourceful.
  • 伪饰为男 - ‘伪饰’ literally means ‘to disguise’ or ‘to feign,’ and ‘为男’ means ‘as a male.’ This expression refers to the act of disguising oneself as a man, often for survival or social necessity.
  • 挈之以行 - ‘挈’ means ‘to carry’ or ‘to take along,’ and ‘以行’ means ‘to travel.’ This phrase indicates that the father took the child with him on his journeys.
  • 改姓名曰张胜 - ‘改姓名’ means ‘to change one’s name,’ and ‘曰张胜’ means ‘called Zhang Sheng.’ This is a common practice in classical Chinese literature when a character changes identity or assumes a new role.
  • 同卧起 - A phrase meaning ‘sleep and rise together,’ indicating close proximity and shared living arrangements.
  • 有疾 - A general term for illness or ailment.
  • 不去衫袴 - ‘衫’ is a type of robe or upper garment, and ‘袴’ is trousers or pants. This phrase means ‘did not remove the robe and trousers,’ indicating that the person was not undressed in front of the other.
  • 溲溺必待夜 - ‘溲溺’ refers to urination and defecation, and ‘必待夜’ means ‘only at night.’ This implies that the person avoided using the bathroom during the day to conceal their gender.
  • 履袜 - ‘履’ is shoes, and ‘袜’ is socks. This phrase refers to footwear and undergarments.
  • 英初不知为女子也 - ‘不知为女子’ means ‘did not know she was a woman.’ This is a key point in the narrative, showing the concealment of identity.
  • 候其姊 - ‘候’ means ‘to visit’ or ‘to await,’ and ‘其姊’ means ‘her sister.’ This indicates that she was visiting her sister.
  • 止有妹耳 - ‘止’ means ‘only,’ and ‘妹’ is ‘younger sister.’ This is a rhetorical statement indicating that she is the only sibling, and thus her sister should recognize her.
  • 音问不通 - ‘音问’ means ‘news’ or ‘communication,’ and ‘不通’ means ‘not reaching.’ This phrase indicates that there was no communication between the two for years.
  • 存亡未审 - ‘存亡’ means ‘alive or dead,’ and ‘未审’ means ‘not yet known.’ This implies uncertainty about the father’s fate.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 弘治癸丑春 - ‘弘治’ is the reign title of the Ming Emperor Zhu Yuzhen (r. 1487–1505), and ‘癸丑’ is a stem-branch calendar year corresponding to 1493. This provides a historical context for the story, situating it in the late 15th century during the Ming dynasty.
  • 金陵城中女子 - ‘金陵’ is the ancient name for Nanjing, a major city in southern China. ‘女子’ means ‘woman’ or ‘female.’ This sets the geographical and social setting of the story.
  • 挟线香行贩江北诸郡 - ‘挟’ means ‘to carry,’ ‘线香’ is ‘incense sticks,’ and ‘行贩’ means ‘to travel and sell.’ This refers to a common trade practice in the Ming dynasty, where merchants traveled between regions to sell goods.
  • 伪饰为男，挈之以行 - This reflects a historical reality in which women, especially those without male relatives, sometimes disguised themselves as men to survive or pursue economic opportunities. This was not uncommon in pre-modern China.
  • 改姓名曰张胜 - This is a common narrative device in classical Chinese fiction, where a character adopts a new identity to escape danger or pursue a new life.
  • 合伙，仍以贩香为业 - ‘合伙’ means ‘to partner up’ or ‘to form a partnership,’ and ‘仍以贩香为业’ means ‘continued in the business of selling incense.’ This reflects the continuation of a traditional trade.
  • 同卧起，但云有疾，不去衫袴 - This reflects the social norms and taboos of the time regarding gender and modesty. The act of sleeping together in the same room but not removing clothing was a way to maintain appearances and conceal identity.
  • 溲溺必待夜 - This again reflects the strict social expectations of modesty and privacy, especially for women.
  • 英初不知为女子也 - This highlights the effectiveness of the disguise and the social constraints of the time, where women were often confined to the domestic sphere.
  • 候其姊 - This reflects the familial structure and the importance of sibling relationships in classical Chinese society.
  • 音问不通，存亡未审 - This reflects the challenges of communication in the pre-modern era, especially for those traveling or living in different regions.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 小說有《錯占凤凰俦》 - A classical reference to a novel, 'The Mistaken Marriage Partner,' which tells a story of mistaken identity or misalliance.
  • 東床已招佳選，何知以羊易牛 - A metaphorical expression comparing a mistaken choice to replacing a cow with a sheep, emphasizing the absurdity of the situation.
  • 西鄰縱有責言，終難指鹿為馬 - Another metaphorical expression, comparing a difficult situation to pointing a horse as a deer, emphasizing the futility of trying to correct a mistaken belief.
  • 兩番渡河，不讓傳書柳毅 - A reference to the legend of Liu Yi, who helped his sister-in-law cross the river, emphasizing the difficulty of the task.
  • 三宵隔被，何慚秉燭雲長 - A metaphorical expression comparing a difficult situation to holding a candle in the dark, emphasizing the need for guidance and support.
  • 風伯為媒，天公作合 - A metaphorical expression comparing the union of two people to a match made by the wind god and the sky god, emphasizing the divine approval of the union.
  • 佳男配了佳婦，兩得其宜 - A formal expression meaning that a good man is matched with a good woman, emphasizing the harmony and appropriateness of the union.
  • 求妻到底無妻，自作之孽 - A formal expression meaning that the man who sought a wife ended up without one, emphasizing the consequences of his own actions.
  • 高氏斷歸錢青，不須另作花燭 - A formal expression meaning that the high family is now given to Qian Qing, and there is no need for a new wedding ceremony, emphasizing the finality of the decision.
  • 顏俊既不合設騙局於前，又不合奮老拳於後 - A formal expression meaning that Yan Jun not only set up a scam beforehand but also hit with a fist afterward, emphasizing the double wrongdoings.
  • 事既不諧，姑免罪責 - A formal expression meaning that since the matter did not work out, he is temporarily exempt from blame, emphasizing the leniency of the judgment.
  • 所費聘金，合助錢青，以贖一擊之罪 - A formal expression meaning that the dowry money should be given to Qian Qing to redeem the sin of hitting him, emphasizing the compensation.
  • 尤辰往來(女扇)誘，實啟釁端，重懲示儆 - A formal expression meaning that You Chen's repeated inducements indeed started the conflict, and he should be severely punished as a warning, emphasizing the severity of the punishment.
  • 沈伯明為作傳奇 - A formal expression meaning that Shen Boming wrote a legend about this, emphasizing the literary treatment of the event.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 小說載此事 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Novels record this matter,' introducing the story to be recounted.
  • 病者為劉璞 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'The patient is Liu Pu,' identifying the main character.
  • 其妹已許字裴九之子裴政矣 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'His sister had been betrothed to Pei Jiu's son Pei Zheng,' explaining the sister's marital status.
  • 璞所聘孫氏，其第孫潤，亦已聘徐雅之女 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Liu Pu's fiancée is Sun, and her brother Sun Run has also been betrothed to Xu Ya's daughter,' detailing the relationships.
  • 而潤以少俊，代姊沖喜，遂與劉妹有私 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'However, Sun Run, being young and handsome, substituted for his sister to bring good fortune, and thus had an affair with Liu's sister,' describing the substitution and subsequent affair.
  • 及經官，官乃使孫劉為配，而以孫所聘徐氏償裴 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'When the matter came before the court, the judge ordered Sun and Liu to marry, and compensated Pei with Xu, whom Sun had been betrothed to,' outlining the judge's decision.
  • 事更奇 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'The matter is even more peculiar,' emphasizing the unusual nature of the case.
  • 其判牒云 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'The judge's edict says,' introducing the judge's reasoning.
  • 弟代姊嫁，姑伴嫂眠 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'The younger brother substitutes for his sister's marriage, while the older sister accompanies her sister-in-law,' using a play on words to highlight the substitution.
  • 愛女愛子，情在理中 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Loving one's daughter and son, the emotions are within reason,' justifying the actions.
  • 一雌一雄，變出意外 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'One male and one female, a change that occurs unexpectedly,' describing the unexpected outcome.
  • 移干柴近烈火，無怪其燃 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Moving dry firewood close to a flame, it is not surprising that it burns,' using a metaphor to explain the affair.
  • 以美玉配明珠，適獲其偶 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Matching a fine jade with a precious pearl, finding the right match,' justifying the judge's decision.
  • 孫氏子因姊而得婦，搂处子不用逾墙 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Sun's son gains a wife through his sister, without needing to climb over walls,' explaining the marriage.
  • 劉氏女因嫂而得夫，懷吉士初非衒玉 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Liu's daughter gains a husband through her sister-in-law, and the virtuous man was not boasting about his jade,' justifying the marriage.
  • 相悅為婿，禮以義起 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Mutual affection leads to marriage, and the ceremony is based on righteousness,' explaining the moral basis of the marriage.
  • 所厚者薄，事可權宜 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'What is valued may be slight, but the matter can be handled flexibly,' justifying the judge's flexibility.
  • 使徐雅别婿裴九之兒，許裴政改娶孫郎之配 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Let Xu Ya's daughter marry another son of Pei Jiu, and allow Pei Zheng to remarry Sun's son's wife,' outlining the compensation.
  • 奪人婦，人亦奪其婦，兩家恩怨，總息风波 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Taking someone's wife, others also take their wives, the grievances of both families are settled,' explaining the resolution of the conflict.
  • 獨樂樂，不若與人樂 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Enjoying alone is not as good as enjoying with others,' emphasizing the benefits of shared happiness.
  • 三對夫妻，各谐鱼水 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Three pairs of couples, each harmonious,' describing the final outcome.
  • 人雖兑换，十六兩原只一斤 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'People may be exchanged, but sixteen taels are still one catty,' using a metaphor to explain the fairness of the exchange.
  • 親是交門，五百年必非錯配 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'The families are now interrelated, and after five hundred years, it is not a mistaken match,' emphasizing the long-term harmony.
  • 以愛及愛，伊父母自作冰人 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Loving one leads to loving another, the parents themselves act as matchmakers,' justifying the parents' role.
  • 非親是親，我官府權為月老 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Not being related becomes related, the official acts as a matchmaker,' justifying the judge's role.
  • 已經明斷，各赴良期 - Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'The matter has been clearly decided, and each couple should proceed to their good fortune,' concluding the judgment.

  • 唯一庄在宋城南 - '唯一' means 'only,' '庄' means 'homestead' or 'farm,' and '宋城南' means 'south of Songcheng.' Together, it means 'there was only a homestead in the south of Songcheng.'
  • 与乳母陈氏居 - '与' means 'with,' '乳母' means 'nursemaid,' and '陈氏' is the name of the nursemaid. Together, it means 'I lived with the nursemaid Chen.'
  • 去店近 - '去' means 'distance,' and '店' means 'shop' or 'store.' Together, it means 'close to the shop.'
  • 鬻蔬以给朝夕 - '鬻' means 'sell,' '蔬' means 'vegetables,' '以' means 'to,' and '给朝夕' means 'to support daily life.' Together, it means 'selling vegetables to support daily life.'
  • 陈氏怜 - '怜' means 'pity' or 'compassion.'
  • 不忍暂弃 - '不忍' means 'unable to bear,' and '暂弃' means 'to abandon temporarily.' Together, it means 'unable to bear to abandon temporarily.'
  • 三岁时 - Means 'at the age of three.'
  • 抱行市中 - '抱' means 'carry,' '行' means 'walk,' and '市中' means 'in the market.' Together, it means 'carried and walked in the market.'
  • 为狂贼所刺 - '为' means 'by,' '狂贼' means 'mad bandit,' and '所刺' means 'stabbed.' Together, it means 'stabbed by a mad bandit.'
  • 刀痕尚在 - '刀痕' means 'knife wound,' and '尚在' means 'still present.' Together, it means 'the knife wound is still there.'
  • 故以花子覆之 - '故' means 'therefore,' '以' means 'with,' '花子' means 'flower ornament,' and '覆之' means 'covered it.' Together, it means 'therefore, she covered it with a flower ornament.'
  • 七八年间 - Means 'for several years.'
  • 叔从事卢龙 - '叔' means 'uncle,' '从事' means 'to be employed,' and '卢龙' is the name of a place. Together, it means 'my uncle was employed in Lulong.'
  • 遂得在左右 - '遂' means 'therefore,' '得' means 'could,' and '在左右' means 'by his side.' Together, it means 'therefore, I could be by his side.'
  • 以为女嫁君耳 - '以为' means 'thought,' '女' means 'daughter,' '嫁' means 'married,' and '君' means 'you.' Together, it means 'thought she was my daughter and married you.'
  • 何以知之 - '何以' means 'how,' '知' means 'know,' and '之' is a pronoun. Together, it means 'How do you know?'
  • 因尽言之 - '因' means 'therefore,' '尽' means 'fully,' and '言之' means 'explained it.' Together, it means 'therefore, he fully explained it.'
  • 相敬愈极 - '相敬' means 'mutual respect,' and '愈极' means 'increased greatly.' Together, it means 'mutual respect increased greatly.'
  • 后生男鲲 - '后' means 'later,' '生' means 'gave birth to,' and '男鲲' is the name of the son. Together, it means 'later, she gave birth to a son named Kun.'
  • 为雁门太守 - '为' means 'became,' '雁门' is the name of a place, and '太守' means 'governor.' Together, it means 'became the governor of Yanmen.'
  • 封太原郡太夫人 - '封' means 'bestowed,' '太原郡' is the name of a place, and '太夫人' means 'lady.' Together, it means 'bestowed the title of Lady of Taiyuan.'
  • 知阴骘之定，不可变也 - '知' means 'knew,' '阴骘' means 'fate' or 'destiny,' '之定' means 'is fixed,' and '不可变也' means 'cannot be changed.' Together, it means 'knew that fate is fixed and cannot be changed.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 勢家慕其高節 - Families of high status admired his noble character.
  • 多欲女之 - Many wanted to marry their daughters to him. The phrase '多欲女之' is an elliptical construction where '女之' means 'to marry their daughters to him.'
  • 鴻並不受 - Huang did not accept any of them. This indicates Huang's rejection of all marriage proposals.
  • 肥丑而黑 - Fat, ugly, and dark-skinned. This is a straightforward description of Sheng's appearance.
  • 力舉石臼 - Strong enough to lift a stone mortar. This phrase describes Sheng's physical strength.
  • 擇對不嫁 - She chose not to marry. This indicates Sheng's decision to remain unmarried.
  • 欲得賢如梁伯鸾者 - She wanted to marry someone as virtuous as Lian. This shows Sheng's high standards for a husband.
  • 吾欲裘褐之人可与俱隐深山者 - I want someone who can wear coarse clothing and live in the mountains with me. This expresses Huang's desire for a simple and rustic lifestyle.
  • 今衣绮缟，博粉墨 - Now you are dressed in fine silk and makeup. This contrasts Huang's ideal with Sheng's current appearance.
  • 岂鸿所愿哉 - Is this what I desire? This rhetorical question emphasizes Huang's dissatisfaction with Sheng's appearance.
  • 以观夫子之志耳 - To see your true intentions. This explains why Sheng initially presented herself in a more conventional manner.
  • 更为椎髻，著布衣 - She then tied her hair in a simple bun and wore plain clothes. This describes Sheng's transformation to match Huang's ideal.
  • 操作而前 - She went before him with her work. This indicates Sheng's readiness to perform household tasks.
  • 此真梁鸿妻也 - This is truly the wife of Lian. This is Huang's recognition of Sheng as his ideal partner.
  • 字之曰德耀，名孟光 - He named her De Yao, and her surname was Meng. This describes the naming of Sheng by Huang.
  • 欲相与入霸陵山中，以耕织自食 - They wanted to go to the mountains of Baling and live by farming and weaving. This expresses their desire for a simple, self-sufficient life.
  • 为人赁舂 - They worked as hired laborers. This indicates their occupation.
  • 妻具食，举案必齐眉 - Her wife prepared food and served it with the tray held level with her eyebrows. This describes Sheng's respectful and diligent service.
  • 被佣能使其妻敬之如此，非常人 - A person who hires laborers and can make his wife respect him like this is not an ordinary person. This highlights Gao Botong's recognition of Huang's character.
  • 乃舍之于家 - So he let them stay in his home. This describes Gao Botong's decision to provide them with shelter.
