Idiomatic Expressions

  • 長卿氏曰 - A citation format where '長卿氏' is likely a pseudonym or collective name for a school of thought, similar to '子曰' (Confucius said). Introduces a statement or commentary.
  • 肥黑而丑之女 - Describes a woman who is dark-skinned, plump, and considered unattractive. Emphasizes her physical appearance.
  • 衣绮缟，博粉墨 - '衣绮缟' means wearing fine silk garments, and '博粉墨' means applying makeup or powder. Together, they describe the adornment of the woman.
  • 设以身当之 - A hypothetical scenario where the woman herself would be the subject of the action. Suggests a personal experience or hypothetical situation.
  • 夫有所受之也 - A concluding phrase that implies the woman has something to gain or benefit from the situation. '夫' introduces a conclusion or summary.
  • 钟离春黄头深目，长肚大节，昂鼻结喉，肥项少发，折腰出胸，皮肤若漆 - A detailed description of the physical appearance of Zhongli Chun, emphasizing her distinctive and unconventional features. '黄头深目' (yellow hair and deep-set eyes), '长肚大节' (long belly and large joints), '昂鼻结喉' (upturned nose and thick neck), '肥项少发' (fat neck with sparse hair), '折腰出胸' (bent waist and protruding chest), and '皮肤若漆' (skin like lacquer) all contribute to her unique appearance.
  • 行年三十，无所容人 - Means 'at the age of thirty, she had no place in society.' Highlights her social isolation.
  • 衒嫁不售 - Means 'she tried to sell herself as a bride but failed.' Metaphorical expression suggests her inability to find a husband.
  • 乃自诣齐宣，乞备后宫 - Means 'she went to King Qi Xuan and requested to be included in the harem.' Describes her bold action.
  • 乃说王以四殆 - Means 'she then advised the king about four dangers.' Indicates her role as a wise counselor.
  • 王拜为后 - Means 'the king made her his queen.' Shows the outcome of her advice.
  • 此丑妇求夫诀也 - A concluding phrase that summarizes the story as a lesson about how an unattractive woman can find success through wisdom and courage.
  • 此法一传而为桓少君 - Means 'this method was passed down to Huan Shaoshun.' Indicates the continuation of the story.
  • 少君归鲍宣，妆送甚盛 - Means 'Shaoshun returned to Bo Xuan with a grand dowry.' Describes the initial situation.
  • 宣不悦曰 - Means 'Xuan was unhappy and said.' Introduces his reaction.
  • 少君曰 - Means 'Shaoshun said.' Introduces her response.
  • 大人以先生修德守行，故使贱妾侍巾栉 - Means 'Your father chose you because of your virtue and conduct, so I, a lowly wife, serve you.' Shows her humility and respect.
  • 既奉承君子，唯命是从 - Means 'Now that I serve you, I will follow your commands.' Indicates her submission.
  • 乃悉归侍御服饰，共著短布裳 - Means 'She then returned all her dowry and dressed in simple clothing.' Shows her commitment to simplicity.
  • 与宣共挽鹿车，归乡里 - Means 'She and Xuan pulled a cart together and returned to their hometown.' Describes their life together.
  • 拜姑礼毕，提瓮出汲 - Means 'After performing the ritual for her mother-in-law, she carried a water jar to draw water.' Shows her diligence and humility.
  • 再传而为袁隗妻马伦 - Means 'This method was passed down again to Ma Lun, wife of Yuan Wei.' Indicates the continuation of the story.
  • 伦是融女，家势丰豪 - Means 'Lun is the daughter of Rong, and her family is wealthy and powerful.' Describes her background.
  • 妆遣甚盛 - Means 'She was sent with a grand dowry.' Describes the initial situation.
  • 隗问曰 - Means 'Wei asked.' Introduces his reaction.
  • 妇奉箕帚而已，何乃过珍丽乎 - Means 'A wife only needs to sweep and carry water, why such luxury?' Questions the extravagance of the dowry.
  • 对曰 - Means 'She replied.' Introduces her response.
  • 慈母垂爱，不敢逆命 - Means 'My mother-in-law loves me dearly, and I dare not disobey her.' Shows her respect and obedience.
  • 君若欲慕鲍宣、梁鸿之高，妾亦愿从少君、孟光之事矣 - Means 'If you wish to emulate Bo Xuan and Liang Hong, I will also follow the example of Shaoshun and Meng Guang.' Shows her willingness to live a simple life.
  • 此富家女降夫入门诀也 - A concluding phrase that summarizes the story as a lesson about how a wealthy woman can adapt to a simpler life through humility and obedience.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 越三日 - Temporal phrase meaning 'three days later' or 'after three days.'
  • 玉娘从内出 - Literally 'Juyuan came out from within,' referring to Juyuan emerging from inside the house.
  • 见万里面有泪痕 - Literally 'saw Wanli with tear marks,' indicating that Wanli was crying.
  • 知其怀乡 - Literally 'knew he was homesick,' showing that Juyuan understood Wanli's longing for home.
  • 观君才品，必非久于人下者 - Literally 'observed your talent and character, you are certainly not one who can stay long under others,' suggesting that Juyuan believed Wanli had great potential and should not remain in a subordinate position.
  • 何不早图脱网，而自甘仆隶乎 - Literally 'why not plan early to escape the net and not willingly become a servant?' This is a metaphorical way of saying that Wanli should take action to free himself from his current situation.
  • 心念，此殆万户遣试我也 - Literally 'thought to himself, this must be a test sent by the thousand-household lord,' indicating that Wanli suspected Juyuan's advice was a test.
  • 妇人必不及此 - Literally 'a woman would not think of such things,' implying that Juyuan's advice was too sophisticated for a woman to come up with.
  • 明日以玉娘之言告万户 - Literally 'the next day, he told the thousand-household lord what Juyuan had said.'
  • 万户怒，欲挞玉娘 - Literally 'the thousand-household lord was angry and wanted to beat Juyuan.'
  • 其妻解之而止 - Literally 'his wife intervened and stopped him,' showing that the wife prevented the thousand-household lord from punishing Juyuan.
  • 玉娘全无怨色 - Literally 'Juyuan showed no sign of resentment,' indicating that Juyuan remained calm and composed.
  • 万里愈疑 - Literally 'Wanli became even more suspicious,' showing that Wanli's suspicions grew.
  • 是晚，玉娘复以为言，词益苦 - Literally 'that evening, Juyuan again spoke these words, with even more earnestness,' indicating that Juyuan repeated her advice with greater urgency.
  • 及明，万里复告之 - Literally 'by morning, Wanli repeated the message,' showing that Wanli informed the thousand-household lord again.
  • 万户乃鬻玉娘于人为妾 - Literally 'the thousand-household lord then sold Juyuan to someone as a concubine,' indicating that Juyuan was sold into slavery.
  • 许万里以别娶 - Literally 'promised Wanli that he could marry someone else,' showing that the thousand-household lord offered Wanli a way out of his situation.
  • 万里至是始自恨负此忠告 - Literally 'Wanli only then began to regret not heeding this loyal advice,' indicating that Wanli realized too late that he should have listened to Juyuan.
  • 然已无及矣 - Literally 'but it was too late,' showing that the opportunity had passed.
  • 玉娘临行，以绣鞋一只，易其夫旧履，怀之，以为异日萍水之券 - Literally 'as Juyuan was leaving, she exchanged her embroidered shoe for her husband's old shoes, kept them in her heart, and used them as a token for future encounters,' indicating that Juyuan kept a memento of her husband.
  • 自是万里为主人委任不忌，竟以其间，窃善马南奔 - Literally 'from then on, Wanli was entrusted with no reservations by his master, and eventually, he stole a good horse and fled south,' showing that Wanli took advantage of his newfound trust to escape.
  • 至临安，值度宗方立，录用先世苗裔 - Literally 'he arrived in Lin'an, just as Emperor Du Zong was enthroned, and he was recruited as a descendant of a distinguished family,' indicating that Wanli was recognized and given a position due to his lineage.
  • 万里上书自陈，补福清尉，历官闽中安抚使 - Literally 'Wanli submitted a petition, was appointed as the magistrate of Fucheng, and later served as the governor of Fujian,' showing Wanli's rise in officialdom.

  • 微而婿之，贵而弃之 - '微' means 'humble' or 'lowly', '贵' means 'noble' or 'wealthy'. Describes a situation where someone who was initially humble was married, but once they became wealthy, they were abandoned.
  • 其妇何罪 - Means 'what crime has her husband committed?'

  • 假今为子，其不为刘叟之见笞者几何 - '假今' means 'if now' or 'suppose now', '刘叟' refers to an elderly man named Liu, '见笞' means 'to be beaten by'. Suggests that if he were a son, how many times would he not be beaten by the elderly man Liu?
  • 天遣运转使为结此一段薄情公案 - '天遣' means 'sent by heaven', '运转使' can be a title or a metaphorical term, '薄情' means 'cold-hearted' or 'unfeeling'. Suggests that heaven has sent someone to resolve this matter of cold-heartedness.
  • 严武、王魁之报，恐不免矣 - '严武' and '王魁' are historical figures known for their cold-heartedness, '报' means 'retribution' or 'punishment'. Suggests that retribution for such cold-heartedness is inevitable.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 江浙 - Refers to the Jiangnan region, encompassing modern-day Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces, known for their cultural and economic significance during the classical period.
  • 永嘉崔尉 - Yongjia (永嘉) is a historical county in modern-day Zhejiang province. '崔尉' refers to Cui, the magistrate or official in charge of a district.
  • 正室 - Reflects the hierarchical nature of traditional Chinese family structures, where the principal wife (正室) held authority over concubines (妾).
  • 中秋赏月 - The Mid-Autumn Festival is a major traditional Chinese holiday, celebrated with moon worship, family reunions, and eating mooncakes.
  • 白衣大士 - This is a common name for Guanyin (Avalokiteshvara), the Bodhisattva of Compassion, often depicted in white robes. The term reflects the Buddhist cultural context.
  • 内典 - This term refers specifically to the Buddhist canon, distinguishing it from Confucian or Daoist texts.
  • 院主 - This is a term used in Chinese Buddhist monasteries to denote the head monk or abbot.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 歲余 - Literally 'after a year,' but in Classical Chinese, it can be used to indicate a vague passage of time, not necessarily exactly one year.
  • 忽有人至院随喜 - '忽然有人来到院中随喜' – '随喜' is a Buddhist term meaning to visit a monastery or temple out of good will, to partake in the merit or spiritual atmosphere.
  • 留斋而去 - 'left after staying in the斋 (a meditation hall or a place for study and reflection).'
  • 将画芙蓉一幅来施 - 'brought a painting of a lotus to make a donation.' '施' here refers to a charitable offering or donation, often in a religious or spiritual context.
  • 老尼张于素屏 - 'the old nun hung it on a plain screen.' '素屏' is a plain or unadorned screen, often used in monastic settings.
  • 王过见之，识为英笔 - 'the official passed by and saw it, recognizing it as a masterful brushwork.' '英笔' refers to excellent or refined brushwork, a term used to praise the skill of a painter or calligrapher.
  • 因询其所自 - 'and inquired about its origin.' '所自' is a classical construction meaning 'its source' or 'where it came from.'
  • 院主曰：“近有檀越布施。” - 'The abbess said, 'Recently, a benefactor made a donation.' '檀越' is a term of respect used to refer to a donor or patron, especially in Buddhist contexts.
  • 默识之 - 'He then silently memorized it.' '默识' means to memorize silently or to take note in one's mind.
  • 援笔题于屏上 - 'took up his brush and inscribed on the screen.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 随喜 - A Buddhist term used to describe visiting a religious site or participating in its activities out of good will, often associated with accumulating merit.
  • 老尼 - An older nun or abbess, indicating a monastic setting.
  • 素屏 - A plain or unadorned screen, commonly used in monastic settings for hanging scrolls or paintings.
  • 檀越 - A respectful term for a benefactor or patron, especially in Buddhist contexts, often used to refer to donors of religious or spiritual works.
  • 操舟为业 - 'To make a living by operating boats,' indicating a profession on the water, possibly as a ferryman, fisherman, or smuggler.
  • 劫掠江湖间 - 'Plundering the rivers and lakes.' '江湖' refers to rivers and lakes, but in classical usage, it can also be a metaphor for the lawless or chaotic regions, often associated with bandits or outlaws.
  • 王 - Likely refers to a local official or magistrate, given the context of a painting being donated and a question about the donor.
  • 院主 - The head or abbess of a Buddhist monastery or nunnery.

Proper Nouns

  • 王 - Likely refers to a local official or magistrate, though the name is not given. In translation, this should be rendered as 'the official' or 'the magistrate.'
  • 院主 - The head of the monastery or nunnery, likely a nun, referred to as 'the abbess.'
  • 顾阿秀兄弟 - The name of the brothers who made the donation. '顾阿秀' is a proper name, and '兄弟' means 'brothers.'
  • 芙蓉 - The lotus flower, a common subject in Chinese painting and poetry, symbolizing purity and enlightenment.
  • 素屏 - A plain screen, likely a bamboo or paper screen used for hanging scrolls in monastic settings.

  • 生为王瑚，妓为陈林春，商为周镗，奸夫莫有良 - A list introducing the main characters of the story, where '生' (shēng) refers to the man, '妓' (jì) to the courtesan, '商' (shāng) to the merchant, and '奸夫' (jiān fū) to the adulterer. '莫有良' (mò yǒu liáng) means 'has no good qualities.'

  • 瓦砾可为金玉，缘在不问良贱也 - A phrase using a contrast between broken tiles and precious metals to illustrate the idea that destiny does not care about value.
  • 或百求而不获，或无心而自至 - A phrase describing two different ways in which destiny can manifest.
  • 或久睽而复合，或欲割而终联 - A phrase describing two different ways in which relationships can develop.
  • 缘定于天，情亦阴受其转而不知矣 - A phrase emphasizing the idea that destiny is determined by heaven and subtly influences love.
  • 虽至无情，不能强缘之断 - A phrase emphasizing the idea that even if one is heartless, they cannot break destiny.
  • 虽至多情，不能强缘之合 - A phrase emphasizing the idea that even if one is very emotional, they cannot make destiny come together.
  • 诚知缘不可强也 - A phrase emphasizing the idea that destiny cannot be forced.
  • 多情者，固不必取盈，而无情者，亦胡为甘自菲薄耶 - A phrase emphasizing that those who are emotional do not need to be excessive, and those who are heartless should not belittle themselves.

  • 申州请旨 - Phrase meaning 'The prefecture requested an imperial decree,' where '申' means 'request' and '请旨' means 'seeking an imperial order.'
  • 邑方逮女出官，中途而返 - Phrase meaning 'The county was about to take the girl out of office, but she returned halfway,' where '逮' means 'arrest' and '出官' means 'to be released from office.'
  • 太守得湖帅使书，而本县申文亦至 - Phrase meaning 'The prefect received a letter from the governor of Hubei, and the county's petition also arrived,' where '使书' means 'letter from an envoy' and '申文' means 'petition.'
  • 辛氏以本县擅释张子，赴州陈诉 - Phrase meaning 'Xin's family accused the county of releasing Zhang without authorization and went to the prefecture to lodge a complaint,' where '擅释' means 'released without permission.'
  • 太守晓辛曰 - Phrase meaning 'The prefect explained to Xin,' where '晓' means 'explain' or 'make clear.'
  • 罗氏不廉女也 - Phrase meaning 'The family of Luo is not corrupt,' where '不廉' means 'not corrupt' or 'integrity.'
  • 天下多美妇人，汝焉用此为 - Phrase meaning 'There are many beautiful women in the world, why do you need this one?' where '焉用' means 'why do you need.'
  • 当今罗氏还尔聘财 - Phrase meaning 'The family of Luo will return your dowry,' where '聘财' means 'dowry.'
  • 辛辞塞 - Phrase meaning 'Xin had no reply,' where '辞塞' means 'unable to respond.'
  • 太守令吏取辛情愿休亲状，行移本县，追理聘财 - Phrase meaning 'The prefect ordered the clerk to obtain a statement from Xin expressing his willingness to divorce, and sent it to the county to recover the dowry,' where '行移' means 'send' and '追理' means 'recover.'
  • 密书与宰，令为张罗，了此一段姻缘 - Phrase meaning 'The prefect secretly wrote to the magistrate, instructing him to arrange the marriage for Zhang, and resolve this matter of marriage,' where '了此一段姻缘' means 'resolve this matter of marriage.'
  • 宰具札招罗仁卿公厅相见，即贺其得佳婿，盛礼特筵，具道守意 - Phrase meaning 'The magistrate prepared a formal letter inviting Luo Renqing to meet at the public hall, congratulating him on finding a good son-in-law, and prepared a grand feast to explain the prefect's intentions,' where '具札' means 'prepare a formal letter' and '特筵' means 'grand feast.'
  • 罗归，招张来赘 - Phrase meaning 'Luo returned home and invited Zhang to come and live in her household,' where '赘' means 'to live in a woman's household.'
  • 张明年登科，仕至倅 - Phrase meaning 'Zhang passed the imperial examination the following year and was appointed as a deputy magistrate,' where '登科' means 'pass the imperial examination' and '仕至倅' means 'appointed as a deputy magistrate.'
  • 夫妇偕老焉 - Phrase meaning 'They lived together until old age,' where '偕老' means 'live together until old age.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 羅帶同心結到成 - Traditional symbol of love and commitment, often used in classical Chinese literature to represent the bond between lovers.
  • 辛氏有巨赀 - Refers to the wealth of the Xin family, which played a significant role in the legal proceedings.
  • 湖北帅 - Refers to the governor of Hubei province, a high-ranking official who influenced the case.
  • 旗铃就圄中报捷 - Formal ritual to announce good news, often used in official settings.
  • 申州请旨 - Refers to the process of requesting an imperial decree, which was a common practice in imperial China.
  • 太守 - Refers to the prefect, a high-ranking local official responsible for administrative and judicial matters.
  • 辛氏以本县擅释张子，赴州陈诉 - Refers to the legal system in imperial China, where complaints could be lodged with higher authorities.
  • 辛辞塞 - Refers to the inability to provide a satisfactory response, often used in legal or diplomatic contexts.
  • 太守令吏取辛情愿休亲状 - Refers to the legal document used to formally request a divorce.
  • 密书与宰 - Refers to the secret communication between officials, often used to coordinate actions behind the scenes.
  • 张明年登科，仕至倅 - Refers to the imperial examination system and the career path of officials in imperial China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 生之及第做官人 - Compound phrase referring to someone who passed the imperial examinations and became an official.
  • 不先不后，恰在圄中 - Idiom suggesting that the event happened at just the right time, neither too early nor too late.
  • 文昌主婚，朱衣人作媒 - Metaphors for the role of the gods and officials in the success of a marriage or career.
  • 一场丑事，反为美谈 - Describes how an initially scandalous or embarrassing event turned into a topic of admiration or discussion.
  • 向使罗父母不觉 - '向使' (xiang shi) means 'if it were not for' or 'had it not been for.' '罗父母' (luo fu mu) refers to the parents of Luo.
  • 两人者终当以情死 - '终当以情死' (zhong dang yi qing si) means 'would ultimately have died due to their feelings.'
  • 颠之倒之，造物真巧于簸弄哉 - '颠之倒之' (dian zhi dao zhi) means 'upside down' or 'inverted.' '造物' (zao wu) refers to the creator or nature. '簸弄' (bo nong) means 'to play with' or 'to manipulate.'
  • 隐于谷里，显于澧浔 - '隐于谷里' (yin yu gu li) means 'hidden in the valley.' '显于澧浔' (xian yu li xun) means 'revealed in the river.'
  • 贵比于白玉，重匹于黄金 - '贵比于白玉' (gui bi yu bai yu) means 'as precious as white jade.' '重匹于黄金' (zhong pi yu huang jin) means 'as heavy as gold.'
  • 既入燕姬之梦，还鸣宋玉之琴 - '既入燕姬之梦' (ji ru yan ji zhi meng) means 'entered the dream of Yan's concubine.' '还鸣宋玉之琴' (huan ming song yu zhi qin) means 'also played the music of Song Yu's qin.'
  • 少与邻生文茂笔札周旋 - '少与邻生文茂笔札周旋' (shao yu lin sheng wen mao bi zha zhou xuan) means 'when she was young, she exchanged letters and writings with her neighbor Wen Mao.'
  • 每自誓言，当为伉俪 - '每自誓言，当为伉俪' (mei zi shi yan, dang wei kang li) means 'she often vowed to herself that they would be husband and wife.'
  • 及长而散去 - '及长而散去' (ji chang er san qu) means 'when they grew up, they went their separate ways.'
  • 犹时时托侍女通殷勤 - '犹时时托侍女通殷勤' (you shi shi tuo shi nu tong yin qin) means 'she still occasionally entrusted her maid to convey her affection.'
  • 茂尝春日寄以诗曰 - '茂尝春日寄以诗曰' (mao chang chun ri ji yi shi yue) means 'Wen Mao once sent her a poem on a spring day.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 及第 - Refers to passing the imperial examinations, which was a significant achievement in ancient China.
  • 做官人 - Refers to becoming an official, which was a prestigious career path.
  • 大历中 - Refers to the Dali era, a period during the Tang Dynasty known for its cultural and literary achievements.
  • 小字试莺 - A metaphor for testing one's talent, often used in classical Chinese literature.
  • 女子中之有文者也 - Highlights the rarity of educated women in ancient China.
  • 与母独居 - Reflects the common practice of women living with their mothers, especially if they were unmarried.

Proper Nouns

  • 晁采 - The name of a female character.
  • 大历 - Era name of the Tang Dynasty; rendered as Dali.
  • 试莺 - Nickname or pet name; rendered as 'testing the nightingale.'
  • 尼 - Refers to a nun or a Buddhist nun.
  • 罗父母 - Parents of Luo.
  • 文茂 - Neighbor of Chao Cai.
  • 燕姬 - Concubine of Yan.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 花笺制叶寄郎边 - Poetic expression meaning 'crafting flowered notes and leaves to send to my beloved.'
  • 的的寻鱼为妾传 - Metaphorical expression. '的的' means 'clearly' or 'certainly.' '寻鱼' may be a play on words or a metaphor for seeking a message or communication. '为妾传' means 'to send to me (the concubine).'
  • 并蒂已看灵鹊报 - Poetic reference to *并蒂莲* (twin lotus flowers), symbolizing a harmonious or romantic union. *灵鹊报* refers to the *magpie*, which in Chinese culture is a harbinger of good news or love.
  • 倩郎早觅买花船 - Direct request to the beloved. *倩郎* means 'beg the beloved' or 'ask my beloved to.' *觅买花船* is a metaphor for preparing for a romantic or celebratory occasion.
  • 荏苒至秋 - Poetic expression meaning 'time passed slowly until autumn.' *荏苒* conveys the idea of time passing gradually or imperceptibly.
  • 屡通音问 - 'Frequently exchanged messages' or 'often communicated.' It implies regular and ongoing communication between the lovers.
  • 欢好无由 - 'No opportunity for joy' or 'no chance for intimacy.' It suggests that despite their communication, the lovers are unable to meet or be together.
  • 偶值其母有姻席之行 - 'Coincidentally, her mother had a wedding feast.' *姻席* refers to a wedding banquet or a marriage-related gathering.
  • 采即遣人报茂 - *采* and *茂* are proper names (likely of the lovers). *遣人报茂* means 'sent someone to inform Maowei' or 'sent a message to Maowei.'
  • 乘月至门 - 'Rode the moon to the door.' A poetic or metaphorical expression, possibly referring to a romantic or mystical journey.
  • 遂酬夙愿焉 - 'Thus fulfilled a long-held wish.' *夙愿* means a long-standing wish or desire.
  • 晨起整衣，两不忍别 - 'In the morning, they dressed and were both reluctant to part.' This conveys the emotional depth of their parting.
  • 采因自剪鬓发赠茂 - *剪鬓发* is a gesture of love and sacrifice. In classical Chinese, giving someone a lock of one's hair is a symbolic act of devotion or farewell.
  • 好藏青鬓，早缔白头也 - A poetic farewell. *青鬓* refers to black or dark hair (youth), *白头* means 'white hair' (old age). The phrase is a wish for a lifelong union.
  • 金风渐栗 - *金风* refers to the autumn wind, often associated with the metal element in the Five Elements theory. *渐栗* means 'gradually becoming cold' or 'gradually stiffening.'
  • 侍儿 - A female attendant or servant, often used in classical Chinese to refer to a companion or messenger in a noble household.
  • 几上金猊静不焚 - *金猊* is a golden lion-shaped incense burner. The phrase suggests that the incense is not burning, symbolizing the absence of warmth or presence of the beloved.
  • 匡床愁卧对斜曛 - *匡床* is a well-made or elegant bed. *斜曛* refers to the slanting light of the setting sun. The phrase describes a melancholic scene of someone lying in bed, facing the waning light of day.
  • 犀流金镜人何处 - *犀流* may refer to the flowing of time or the reflection in a mirror. *金镜* is a golden mirror, symbolizing clarity or self-reflection. The phrase questions the whereabouts of the beloved, as if reflected in the mirror.
  • 半枕兰香空绿云 - *兰香* is the fragrance of orchids, symbolizing purity and love. *绿云* is a poetic term for clouds or the hair of a woman. The phrase describes the lingering scent of orchids on the pillow, but the beloved is absent, leaving only empty clouds.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 花笺 - In classical Chinese literature, *花笺* refers to beautifully decorated or flower-patterned paper used for writing love letters or poetry.
  • 灵鹊报 - The *magpie* (*灵鹊*) is a traditional Chinese symbol of good fortune and love. In folklore, the arrival of a magpie is considered an omen of happiness, particularly in love and marriage.
  • 买花船 - While not a well-documented historical term, *买花船* may refer to a boat used to purchase flowers, or it could be a poetic or symbolic reference to a romantic journey or a symbolic boat used in a wedding or love ceremony.
  • 青鬓 - Refers to black or dark hair, symbolizing youth and vitality. In classical Chinese poetry, *青鬓* is often contrasted with *白头* (white hair), which represents old age and the passage of time.
  • 白头 - A traditional symbol of lifelong marriage. The phrase '白头偕老' (to grow old together) is a common Chinese expression for a lasting marriage.
  • 兰香 - The fragrance of the *orchid* (*兰*), which is a symbol of elegance, purity, and love in Chinese culture.
  • 金猊 - A *golden lion-shaped incense burner*, used in ancient Chinese homes and temples. It is often associated with the presence of the beloved or the memory of a loved one.
  • 匡床 - A well-made or elegant bed. In classical poetry, beds are often used as symbols of intimacy or the absence of a lover.
  • 犀流金镜 - The *flow of the rhinoceros* or *flow of time* reflected in a golden mirror. This may be a metaphor for the passage of time or the reflection of the self in a mirror, symbolizing longing for the beloved.
  • 绿云 - A poetic term for clouds or the hair of a woman, often used to describe the softness or beauty of a woman's hair.

Proper Nouns

  • 采 - A proper name, likely of one of the lovers.
  • 茂 - A proper name, likely of one of the lovers.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 空腹清吟托袅烟 - Literal meaning: 'clearly reciting poetry with an empty stomach, entrusting oneself to the rising smoke.' Refers to a poetic or meditative state, possibly while smoking or contemplating in solitude.
  • 樊姬春思描红船 - Literal meaning: 'Fanjie's spring thoughts sketch a red boat.' Fanjie was a historical figure known for her wisdom and literary talent. This phrase suggests a romantic or nostalgic scene, where her thoughts are likened to the graceful painting of a red boat, symbolizing beauty and longing.
  • 相逢何必蓝桥路 - Literal meaning: 'Why must we meet at the Blue Bridge Road?' The Blue Bridge is a famous location in Chinese folklore, often associated with love and romance. This line implies that true connection doesn't require a specific place or circumstance.
  • 休负沧波好月天 - Literal meaning: 'Do not辜负 the good moonlit sky over the vast waves.' A poetic way of saying that one should not miss out on the beauty of nature, especially during a serene night by the sea.
  • 自是芳情不恋春 - Literal meaning: 'It is natural for beautiful feelings not to linger in spring.' Suggests that true emotions or desires are not confined to a particular season, implying a deeper, more enduring sentiment.
  • 淮流清浸天边月 - Literal meaning: 'The clear flow of the Huai River bathes the moon at the edge of the sky.' A vivid description of a moonlit night over the river, emphasizing the clarity and beauty of the scene.
  • 比似郎心向我亲 - Literal meaning: 'It is like the gentleman's heart is closer to me.' This line compares the closeness of the moon to the river with the closeness of the man's heart to the woman, suggesting a deep emotional connection.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 樊姬 - A historical figure known for her intelligence and literary talent. She was a concubine of the Chu king during the Warring States period and is often referenced in literature for her wisdom and grace.
  • 蓝桥 - A legendary bridge in Chinese folklore, often associated with love and romance. It is said that lovers would meet at the Blue Bridge, and it has become a symbol of romantic encounters.
  • 沧波 - Refers to the vast, deep waters of the sea or a large river, often used in poetry to evoke a sense of grandeur and tranquility.
  • 淮流 - Refers to the Huai River, one of the major rivers in China, known for its scenic beauty and historical significance.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This couplet describes the pagodas on Tiger Hill and the Buddha lamps seen at night.
  • This couplet describes the act of making an appointment with companions to go to the temple to burn incense and bringing personal items to donate to the monk.
  • This couplet describes the scene at the gate with ships from Dongwu and the poetic imagery of the night.
  • This couplet describes the early bell of Hanshan Temple and the disturbance caused by fishing lights and river maples.
  • This couplet describes the remaining oranges from Dongting Lake and the silver fish from Lize.
  • This couplet describes the delicious flavors of the southeast and the difficulty in offering them to the emperor.
  • This couplet describes the emergence of reed shoots and the blooming of paulownia flowers, with the absence of certain fish.
  • This couplet describes the fishy wind in the morning and the return of the husband with fresh fish.
  • This couplet describes the changing colors of the willows over the years.
  • This couplet uses the willow as a metaphor for the speaker's fragility and the husband's capriciousness.
  • This couplet describes the natural behavior of jade pheasants and mandarin ducks.
  • This couplet expresses the speaker's dislike of the water at the head of the Precious Belt Bridge.
  • This couplet describes a phoenix hairpin with a green silk top, as green as clouds.
  • This couplet describes an eight-character tooth comb, white like silver.
  • This couplet describes leaning against the red gate, looking up and standing.
  • This couplet describes how many heartbroken people come and go.
  • This couplet describes a hundred-foot high tower leaning against the blue sky.
  • This couplet describes the railing being curved, connected to the painted screen.
  • This couplet describes having my own songs from Suzhou.
  • This couplet describes not going to West Lake to sing *Cai Lian*.
